problem_text:
Problem 3. A game of electronics retail competition [4 points]

R&J Electronics and 4th Street Photo are two retailers that have been competing with one another for many years.  Every year, both retailers have a one-day post-Thanksgiving-pre-Christmas sale on the first Friday of December.  Local residents know about this sale, and plan their holiday electronic shopping around this event.  

Historically, both R&J and 4th Street choose a 10%, 20%, or 30% off sale.  The amount of the sale can be different between the two stores, and both stores keep their own sale amount a tightly guarded secret until they publicly announce it in the Thursday edition of the Daily Times, the local newspaper.  Since residents base their shopping decisions on what they read in the paper and only visit one store on Friday, neither R&J nor 4th Street find it profitable to change their sale amounts after the newspaper has been printed.

You are advising R&J Electronics, and they have provided you the profits (in $’000s) that R&J expects each firm to receive for any combination of sale amounts announced.  They are uncertain about a certain number in the matrix, which they have replaced with X.

R&J Electronics
R&J Electronics

10%
20%
30%
10%
50, 35
40, 30
20, 15
20%
52, 20
45, 27
25, 15 
30%
47, 5
35, X
30, 10



	•	[1 pt] Does R&J have any dominated strategies? (If so, dominated by which strategy?) 

10% is R&J’s dominated strategy, and it is dominated by 20%.


	•	[1 pt] What is the highest integer value of X such that both firms choosing 30% is a Nash Equilibrium?

X should not exceed 10. (9 is also fine.)
If X is greater than 10, then given R&J chooses 30%, 4th Street has an incentive to deviate to 20%.


Suppose from now on that X=15.
	•	[1 pt] What is the Nash Equilibrium of this game?

The NE is (20%, 20%).
This can be found by the best-response method, or eliminating dominated strategy.

	•	[1 pt]This year, R&J is thinking about running an early advertising campaign announcing its discount before the Thursday newspaper is printed (and before 4th Street chooses its discount).  You are certain 4th Street Photo is not planning an early announcement of its own, and also you have received credible information that 4th Street Photo will not choose 30%. Draw the game tree for this new game if R&J announces its discount first and 4th Street Photo only chooses either 10% or 20% afterwards. (You DON’T need to upload the game tree.) What is your prediction of the outcome of this new game? 

The outcome is: R&J chooses 10% and then 4th Street chooses 10% also.

The payoff outcome is (50,35), so both firms benefit from R&J announcing its discount first.

given_solution:
Problem 1. Boycotts 
Note: This problem has been a tradition at Yale SOM for many years. It is a rite of passage for SOM economics students. To answer this problem requires no mathematics beyond arithmetic. Read the instructions carefully, and then feel free to talk about this problem with your fellow students. We advise such discussions to prevent you from wasting time. In the end you must see the answer yourself in your own “aha” moment and of course, the written answer must be your own. 
Setup A frequently used instrument of economic coercion is the boycott. For example, a union involved in a serious and protracted conflict with some employer or group of employers may urge all of its sympathizers to stop buying the products of that employer or group, in the hope that the resulting economic losses will provide an incentive for the employers to make concessions. However, experience shows that the coercive power of a boycott is often small. 
In this exercise, you are to use supply and demand analysis as a tool to understand the effects of boycotts. For the sake of concreteness and precision, most of the questions are posed in quantitative terms. However, the goal of the exercise is to develop your understanding of the logic of the market processes involved. Accordingly, if you have difficulty, don't try to overwhelm the problem with calculation -- rethink the economics. 
Throughout the exercise, the following assumptions are to be maintained. First, the market in question is one to which simple supply and demand analysis is applicable: there are numerous buyers and sellers in the market; the good is homogeneous; buyers and sellers seek only to trade on the most advantageous possible terms and, aside from the complications introduced by the boycott, do not care with whom they transact. Second, the identities of buyers and sellers are fixed: in particular, no reselling of the goods takes place. Third, the set of buyers divides sharply into observers and nonobservers of the boycott, while the sellers separate equally sharply into those that are targets of the boycott and those that are not. Boycott observers will not, under any circumstances, buy from a target seller, while non-observers do not care from whom they buy. For simplicity, it is assumed that boycott observers account for the same fraction of demand at every price. Similarly, target sellers account for a certain fraction of the supply at every price. 
Assume initially that in the pre-boycott situation, the market demand curve is described by: 

where  = quantity demanded and P = the price. The market supply curve has equation (with  = quantity supplied): 

except that, at prices of 5 or less, quantity supplied is zero. 
Warmup problem (i): Determine the market equilibrium in the pre-boycott situation. 
Answer: In equilibrium, the price is such that quantity demanded equals quantity supplied: 

Warmup problem (ii): Suppose that buyers representing 70% of the original demand, observe the boycott. If all non-observers buy from target sellers, what is the demand curve facing target sellers? 
Answer: Since observers will not buy from target sellers at any price, the only demand facing target sellers is from non-observers. Since observers and non-observers account for demand in the ratio 0.7:0.3 at every price, the equation of the demand curve facing target sellers is: 


Questions
Consider the three situations characterized by the following table

Observers as Fraction	Target as Fraction
Situation		of Original Demand		of Original Supply 

   A				3/4				 3/4

   B				1/4				 1/4

   C				1/2				 1/4

For each of the three situations, determine the market equilibrium that would arise under boycott conditions.  Give the quantities transacted by observing and non-observing buyers, and by target and non-target sellers.

Drawing on your results, state the general principle which relates the effectiveness of a boycott to the observance fraction and the target fraction.  “Effectiveness,” in this context, means lowering the price to target sellers.  Can you provide some intuition as well?

Hint: Warmup problem (i) supplies all the mathematical knowledge you will need to solve the remaining problems.  What is needed for the rest is an ability to structure the problem, and an understanding of simple economic principles.  The point of the exercise is to determine whether the boycott is effective in any of the three cases above.  To answer this question, you have been given the following information: (1) the market supply curve, (2) the targeted fraction of sellers, (3) the market demand curve, (4) the observers as a fraction of buyers.  Items (1) and (2) imply that it is possible to write down algebraic expressions for the supply curves of targeted and non-targeted sellers.  You can also choose to use Excel to get a sense of how prices get determined in each sub-market.

See PowerPoint for solution.  0.5 pt for getting market-clearing prices for each submarket. 0.5pt for noticing that non-observers will deviate. 1pt for noticing that only situation C “works”. 0.5pt for the general rule.

Problem 2. Gas price

Gas prices have become remarkably high since the beginning of the year 2022. On June 9th, 2022, a gallon of gasoline costs 4.06 dollars---almost 400% higher than it was on the same date of 2020 ($1.1 per gallon). We will apply what we learned about demand and supply to take a look at gas prices. 

Suppose that in 2020, the worldwide daily supply function of gasoline is given by 


The daily demand of gasoline in 2020 is:


where p is the price per gallon of gasoline and q is in the unit of million gallons.

	•	Solve for the market equilibrium for daily gasoline in 2020. What is the equilibrium price? What is the equilibrium quantity?  
	•	In the summer of 2022, people started to return to offices and to traveling. This leads to an outward shift of the demand. Suppose now that the demand becomes: 
                                                                        
In the meantime, the supply-chain problems and the Ukrainian war has led to an inward shift of the supply. Suppose the supply becomes:  What is the new equilibrium price and what is the new equilibrium quantity?

	•	What is the demand elasticity at the equilibrium of 2022? What is the supply elasticity? Which one is more elastic? 




	•	High gas prices have imposed a great burden on consumers. Suppose that the government now wishes to protect consumers by taxing gasoline supply and considers to impose a $0.5 tax per gallon on gas suppliers. What is the equilibrium price and quantity after tax?


	•	Calculate the burden of this proposed tax; what percent of the tax would be “paid” by consumers (Round to nearest percent)? How is this related to your answer in part c)?
Price increase of $0.33 out of $0.5 tax, == 67%. From part c), the demand curve is less elastic than the supply curve at the equilibrium so consumers have more tax burden.


	•	What is the amount of deadweight loss created by this policy? Is this policy a good idea?

Deadweight loss=0.5*0.5*16.67=4.16.
Taxing gas suppliers may not be a good idea consumers end up paying more. Even if the government rebates all tax revenue, there is still a deadweight loss of $4.16M

Problem 3. Tariffs and Import quotas on vegetable fiber
Consider the market for vegetable fiber in the US. The domestic demand for vegetable fiber is given by

where is quantity measured in millions of pounds and P is the price per pound. Vegetable fiber is also produced domestically in the US and the domestic supply curve is given by 

where  is US output in millions of pounds and p is the domestic price per pound. 
	•	Solve for the domestic equilibrium price and quantity of vegetable fiber.

Solution: 40-2P=2/3P P=15 and Q =10.

	•	Vegetable fiber trades in a highly competitive world market at a price of $9 per pound. There is enough availability that the US can import as much as it needs at this price. Do you think US would import vegetable fiber? What would be the amount imported? 

□ Yes, US would import      □ No, US would not import
Quantity imported: ________________________
Solution: 
Yes, US would import. 
US demand at world price of $9 is 40-2*9= 22
The domestic supply at price of $9 is 2P/3 = 6.
Therefore, quantity imported = 16.

	•	In recent years, the US government imposed a $3 import tariff. Under this tariff, what do you predict is the domestic price of vegetable fiber?

US domestic price in regime with import tariffs: _____________________
Ans: $12
	•	What would be the total tariff revenue collected by the Government?
Tariff revenue: ________________________
Ans: Import when price=12: 16-8=8. Tariff revenue=3*8=24. 
	•	Without any further computations, can you predict who would be the gainers and losers from such a tariff? 
Relative to free trade, US domestic producer surplus under the import would … 
□ Increase
□ Decrease
□ Unclear without more information
Increase
US domestic consumer surplus (under the tariff vs free trade) would … 
□ Increase
□ Decrease
□ Unclear without more information
Decrease
Total US domestic surplus (under the tariff vs free trade) would … 
□ Increase
□ Decrease
□ Unclear without more information
Decrease
Why did you conclude this about total surplus? ___________________________________________
__________________________________________________________________________________________________
__________________________________________________________________________________________________
Answer. US domestic producers would be better off under the tariff, US consumers would be worse off, and total surplus would unambiguously decrease as the tariff would introduce deadweight loss. 

	•	(Challenge question:) Under pressure from trading partners, the US is considering elimination of the tariff. In its place, the proposal is to limit imports into the US to 8 million pounds a year. Under this proposal, what would you predict to be the domestic price of vegetable fiber? What will be the amount produced by domestic producers? 

Answer. 
To figure out domestic price, note that demand must equal supply, and the supply will be met partly by domestic producers and partly by world producers (up to 8 million pounds). So in equilibrium under this export quantity constraint, the effective domestic supply becomes Qs=8+2/3p
Domestic price: _____________________
Quantity produced by domestic suppliers: _____________________

40-2P=8+2/3PP=12 
Domestic Q=8

Problem 1. Pet Daycare. Many households either adopted or purchased new fluffy family members during the pandemic. These lovely friends have brought countless joy and love during quarantine. As the society gradually return to normal, business travels, vacations, and even simply commuting to offices have become challenging for new pet parents. Luckily there’s an industry for pet-daycare/pet hotel. 

Suppose that the weekly cost function for pet daycare (where quantity is in number of pets accommodated a week) is


	•	What is the marginal cost function for pet daycare? 
The industry of pet daycare is arguably quite competitive. Entry is almost free, products   are quite undifferentiated, each supplier is small relative to the market.   
	•	In this market, what is the supply function of pet daycare as a function of the market price p for accommodating a pet? (Specify only the range of prices where the quantity supplied is positive)  

	•	Suppose that the demand for pet daycare is given by  What is the (short-run) equilibrium price and quantity? Is it profitable to be a supplier in this industry? What is the profit (if any)? 

	•	Knowing that entry is profitable, many others will join the business of pet daycare. What is the long-run equilibrium price? What about quantity?  

	•	Being in a competitive industry is not ideal. Suppliers always like to have some market power. One way is to create product differentiation. the Pooch hotel, with business locations in Boston ,MA; Chicago, IL; Norwalk, CT; Denver, CO; and Sunnyvale, CA, offers high-quality pet-care services, including presidential suites, webcams for 24/7 monitoring; pet spa; and personal playtime. This captures a new market for high-quality pet daycare. Suppose that the cost function is  
 What is the optimal monopoly price for the Pooch hotel ? What is the profit? Do you expect this market to become competitive in the long run? Why and why not?

Problem 2. Hair salon versioning. A local hair salon in New Haven is re-designing its business plan. The owner has discovered that they can make more money by offering two packages: “Deluxe” and “Basic”. There are two types of customers: Type A customers usually only need to get haircuts, while Type B customers would enjoy other services (wash, conditioning, coloring, ironing, etc.). Suppose that there are 4 type A customers for each type B customer. The reservations values for each type and marginal costs for each version are as follows:
Reservation Values:
Type A (4)
Type B (1)
Basic (MC=20)
$30
$60
Deluxe (MC=50)
$60
$90

The optimal menu has the form of: Get the basic package for  dollars, or get the deluxe package for  dollars. 
	•	What are the constraints that will bind under this menu?

	•	The “willing to buy” constraint: Type A pays their reservation price.
	•	The “buy the right one” constraint: Type B should prefer Deluxe. 
	•	Use the answers in part a), find the prices  and .
	•	Buy the right one constraint  Type A should buy the basic plan and pay , type B should buy the deluxe plan and pay .
	•	Willing to buy constraint 
	•	Buy the right on constraint  (or 59.99)

	•	What is the hair salon’s profit by offering this menu?  
	•	What is the hair salon’s profit if it only serves type B by offering the deluxe package only?

Optimal price for deluxe price only: 90. Profit= (90-50)*1=40.

given_solution:
Problem 3.  Insurance Market. Consider the following simple model of health insurance.  Let there be three types of people, LOW, MEDIUM, and HIGH risk, whose yearly medical expenses are $0 with 95% probability and $25K, $50K, and $100K (respectively) with 5% probability for the LOW, MEDIUM and HIGH groups. All three risk types are equally represented in the population, and all people have an initial wealth level of $100K, and a utility function over wealth of  (where  is measured in thousands of dollars). To be clear, a HIGH risk person therefore has initial wealth of $100K and a 95% chance of $0 medical expenses, but a 5% chance of $100K in medical expenses.    

Insurers only offer the simplest policy, where people pay a premium but no deductible and medical expenditure is fully reimbursed.  Assume that insurers have no costs other than the expected payouts to their policyholders.  

	•	Compute the expected cost to the insurer for each type of customer. Also, compute the highest premium that each type of consumer is willing to pay (according to expected utility).  

Answer: 

Expenditure
Premium
LOW
1,250
1,335
MEDIUM
2,500
2,907
HIGH
5,000
9,750

           Expenditure:





Premium:






	•	If it were required that all people be insured, and insurance is competitive so that premiums equal average cost, what would the premium be? 

            Answer: Average cost is (1/3)*(1250+2500+5000)=2,916.67 


	•	If insurance were optional, and again insurance is competitive so that premiums equal average cost, what premium would insurers charge? And which risk types would choose to buy insurance at that premium? (Hint: Note that they would consider charging one of the six values solved for in part a). 
Answer: 
-Charge $1335: get all three types, average cost is 2916.67. Not EQ’M 
-Charge 2907: get MEDIUM and HIGH. Average cost is 3750. NOT EQ’M. 
-Charge 9750: get only HIGH. Cost is 5000 
	•	But then since insurance is competitive, price comes down to 5000. Only get HIGH. EQ’M.

For the sake of concreteness and precision, most of the questions are posed in quantitative terms. However, the goal of the exercise is to develop your understanding of the logic of the market processes involved. Accordingly, if you have difficulty, don't try to overwhelm the problem with calculation -- rethink the economics. 
Throughout the exercise, the following assumptions are to be maintained. First, the market in question is one to which simple supply and demand analysis is applicable: there are numerous buyers and sellers in the market; the good is homogeneous; buyers and sellers seek only to trade on the most advantageous possible terms and, aside from the complications introduced by the boycott, do not care with whom they transact. Second, the identities of buyers and sellers are fixed: in particular, no reselling of the goods takes place. Third, the set of buyers divides sharply into observers and nonobservers of the boycott, while the sellers separate equally sharply into those that are targets of the boycott and those that are not. Boycott observers will not, under any circumstances, buy from a target seller, while non-observers do not care from whom they buy. For simplicity, it is assumed that boycott observers account for the same fraction of demand at every price. Similarly, target sellers account for a certain fraction of the supply at every price. 
Assume initially that in the pre-boycott situation, the market demand curve is described by: 

where  = quantity demanded and P = the price. The market supply curve has equation (with  = quantity supplied): 

except that, at prices of 5 or less, quantity supplied is zero. 
Warmup problem (i): Determine the market equilibrium in the pre-boycott situation. 
Answer: In equilibrium, the price is such that quantity demanded equals quantity supplied: 

Warmup problem (ii): Suppose that buyers representing 70% of the original demand, observe the boycott. If all non-observers buy from target sellers, what is the demand curve facing target sellers? 
Answer: Since observers will not buy from target sellers at any price, the only demand facing target sellers is from non-observers. Since observers and non-observers account for demand in the ratio 0.7:0.3 at every price, the equation of the demand curve facing target sellers is: 


Questions
Consider the three situations characterized by the following table

Observers as Fraction	Target as Fraction
Situation		of Original Demand		of Original Supply 

   A				3/4				 3/4

   B				1/4				 1/4

   C				1/2				 1/4

For each of the three situations, determine the market equilibrium that would arise under boycott conditions.  Give the quantities transacted by observing and non-observing buyers, and by target and non-target sellers.

Drawing on your results, state the general principle which relates the effectiveness of a boycott to the observance fraction and the target fraction.  “Effectiveness,” in this context, means lowering the price to target sellers.  Can you provide some intuition as well?

Hint: Warmup problem (i) supplies all the mathematical knowledge you will need to solve the remaining problems.  What is needed for the rest is an ability to structure the problem, and an understanding of simple economic principles.  The point of the exercise is to determine whether the boycott is effective in any of the three cases above.  To answer this question, you have been given the following information: (1) the market supply curve, (2) the targeted fraction of sellers, (3) the market demand curve, (4) the observers as a fraction of buyers.  Items (1) and (2) imply that it is possible to write down algebraic expressions for the supply curves of targeted and non-targeted sellers.  You can also choose to use Excel to get a sense of how prices get determined in each sub-market.

See PowerPoint for solution.  0.5 pt for getting market-clearing prices for each submarket. 0.5pt for noticing that non-observers will deviate. 1pt for noticing that only situation C “works”. 0.5pt for the general rule.
problem_text:
Problem 1. Boycotts 
Note: This problem has been a tradition at Yale SOM for many years. It is a rite of passage for SOM economics students. To answer this problem requires no mathematics beyond arithmetic. Read the instructions carefully, and then feel free to talk about this problem with your fellow students. We advise such discussions to prevent you from wasting time. In the end you must see the answer yourself in your own “aha” moment and of course, the written answer must be your own. 
Setup A frequently used instrument of economic coercion is the boycott. For example, a union involved in a serious and protracted conflict with some employer or group of employers may urge all of its sympathizers to stop buying the products of that employer or group, in the hope that the resulting economic losses will provide an incentive for the employers to make concessions. However, experience shows that the coercive power of a boycott is often small. 
In this exercise, you are to use supply and demand analysis as a tool to understand the effects of boycotts. For the sake of concreteness and precision, most of the questions are posed in quantitative terms. However, the goal of the exercise is to develop your understanding of the logic of the market processes involved. Accordingly, if you have difficulty, don't try to overwhelm the problem with calculation -- rethink the economics. 
Throughout the exercise, the following assumptions are to be maintained. First, the market in question is one to which simple supply and demand analysis is applicable: there are numerous buyers and sellers in the market; the good is homogeneous; buyers and sellers seek only to trade on the most advantageous possible terms and, aside from the complications introduced by the boycott, do not care with whom they transact. Second, the identities of buyers and sellers are fixed: in particular, no reselling of the goods takes place. Third, the set of buyers divides sharply into observers and nonobservers of the boycott, while the sellers separate equally sharply into those that are targets of the boycott and those that are not. Boycott observers will not, under any circumstances, buy from a target seller, while non-observers do not care from whom they buy. For simplicity, it is assumed that boycott observers account for the same fraction of demand at every price. Similarly, target sellers account for a certain fraction of the supply at every price. 
Assume initially that in the pre-boycott situation, the market demand curve is described by: 

where  = quantity demanded and P = the price. The market supply curve has equation (with  = quantity supplied): 

except that, at prices of 5 or less, quantity supplied is zero. 
Warmup problem (i): Determine the market equilibrium in the pre-boycott situation. 
 
Warmup problem (ii): Suppose that buyers representing 70% of the original demand, observe the boycott. If all non-observers buy from target sellers, what is the demand curve facing target sellers? 
 

Questions
Consider the three situations characterized by the following table

Observers as Fraction	Target as Fraction
Situation		of Original Demand		of Original Supply 

   A				3/4				 3/4

   B				1/4				 1/4

   C				1/2				 1/4

For each of the three situations, determine the market equilibrium that would arise under boycott conditions.  Give the quantities transacted by observing and non-observing buyers, and by target and non-target sellers.

Drawing on your results, state the general principle which relates the effectiveness of a boycott to the observance fraction and the target fraction.  “Effectiveness,” in this context, means lowering the price to target sellers.  Can you provide some intuition as well?

Hint: Warmup problem (i) supplies all the mathematical knowledge you will need to solve the remaining problems.  What is needed for the rest is an ability to structure the problem, and an understanding of simple economic principles.  The point of the exercise is to determine whether the boycott is effective in any of the three cases above.  To answer this question, you have been given the following information: (1) the market supply curve, (2) the targeted fraction of sellers, (3) the market demand curve, (4) the observers as a fraction of buyers.  Items (1) and (2) imply that it is possible to write down algebraic expressions for the supply curves of targeted and non-targeted sellers.  You can also choose to use Excel to get a sense of how prices get determined in each sub-market.

Problem 2. Gas price

Gas prices have become remarkably high since the beginning of the year 2022. On June 9th, 2022, a gallon of gasoline costs 4.06 dollars---almost 400% higher than it was on the same date of 2020 ($1.1 per gallon). We will apply what we learned about demand and supply to take a look at gas prices. 

Suppose that in 2020, the worldwide daily supply function of gasoline is given by 


The daily demand of gasoline in 2020 is:


where p is the price per gallon of gasoline and q is in the unit of million gallons.

	•	Solve for the market equilibrium for daily gasoline in 2020. What is the equilibrium price? What is the equilibrium quantity?  
	•	In the summer of 2022, people started to return to offices and to traveling. This leads to an outward shift of the demand. Suppose now that the demand becomes: 
                                                                        
In the meantime, the supply-chain problems and the Ukrainian war has led to an inward shift of the supply. Suppose the supply becomes:  What is the new equilibrium price and what is the new equilibrium quantity?

	•	What is the demand elasticity at the equilibrium of 2022? What is the supply elasticity? Which one is more elastic? 




	•	High gas prices have imposed a great burden on consumers. Suppose that the government now wishes to protect consumers by taxing gasoline supply and considers to impose a $0.5 tax per gallon on gas suppliers. What is the equilibrium price and quantity after tax?


	•	Calculate the burden of this proposed tax; what percent of the tax would be “paid” by consumers (Round to nearest percent)? How is this related to your answer in part c)?
Price increase of $0.33 out of $0.5 tax, == 67%. From part c), the demand curve is less elastic than the supply curve at the equilibrium so consumers have more tax burden.


	•	What is the amount of deadweight loss created by this policy? Is this policy a good idea?

Deadweight loss=0.5*0.5*16.67=4.16.
Taxing gas suppliers may not be a good idea consumers end up paying more. Even if the government rebates all tax revenue, there is still a deadweight loss of $4.16M

Problem 1. Tariffs and Import quotas on vegetable fiber
Consider the market for vegetable fiber in the US. The domestic demand for vegetable fiber is given by

where is quantity measured in millions of pounds and P is the price per pound. Vegetable fiber is also produced domestically in the US and the domestic supply curve is given by 

where  is US output in millions of pounds and p is the domestic price per pound. 
	•	Solve for the domestic equilibrium price and quantity of vegetable fiber.

Solution: 40-2P=2/3P P=15 and Q =10.

	•	Vegetable fiber trades in a highly competitive world market at a price of $9 per pound. There is enough availability that the US can import as much as it needs at this price. Do you think US would import vegetable fiber? What would be the amount imported? 

□ Yes, US would import      □ No, US would not import
Quantity imported: ________________________
Solution: 
Yes, US would import. 
US demand at world price of $9 is 40-2*9= 22
The domestic supply at price of $9 is 2P/3 = 6.
Therefore, quantity imported = 16.

	•	In recent years, the US government imposed a $3 import tariff. Under this tariff, what do you predict is the domestic price of vegetable fiber?

US domestic price in regime with import tariffs: _____________________
Ans: $12
	•	What would be the total tariff revenue collected by the Government?
Tariff revenue: ________________________
Ans: Import when price=12: 16-8=8. Tariff revenue=3*8=24. 
	•	Without any further computations, can you predict who would be the gainers and losers from such a tariff? 
Relative to free trade, US domestic producer surplus under the import would … 
□ Increase
□ Decrease
□ Unclear without more information
Increase
US domestic consumer surplus (under the tariff vs free trade) would … 
□ Increase
□ Decrease
□ Unclear without more information
Decrease
Total US domestic surplus (under the tariff vs free trade) would … 
□ Increase
□ Decrease
□ Unclear without more information
Decrease
Why did you conclude this about total surplus? ___________________________________________
__________________________________________________________________________________________________
__________________________________________________________________________________________________
Answer. US domestic producers would be better off under the tariff, US consumers would be worse off, and total surplus would unambiguously decrease as the tariff would introduce deadweight loss. 

	•	(Challenge question:) Under pressure from trading partners, the US is considering elimination of the tariff. In its place, the proposal is to limit imports into the US to 8 million pounds a year. Under this proposal, what would you predict to be the domestic price of vegetable fiber? What will be the amount produced by domestic producers? 

Answer. 
To figure out domestic price, note that demand must equal supply, and the supply will be met partly by domestic producers and partly by world producers (up to 8 million pounds). So in equilibrium under this export quantity constraint, the effective domestic supply becomes Qs=8+2/3p
Domestic price: _____________________
Quantity produced by domestic suppliers: _____________________

40-2P=8+2/3PP=12 
Domestic Q=8

Problem 2. Pet Daycare. Many households either adopted or purchased new fluffy family members during the pandemic. These lovely friends have brought countless joy and love during quarantine. As the society gradually return to normal, business travels, vacations, and even simply commuting to offices have become challenging for new pet parents. Luckily there’s an industry for pet-daycare/pet hotel. 

Suppose that the weekly cost function for pet daycare (where quantity is in number of pets accommodated a week) is


	•	What is the marginal cost function for pet daycare? 
The industry of pet daycare is arguably quite competitive. Entry is almost free, products   are quite undifferentiated, each supplier is small relative to the market.   
	•	In this market, what is the supply function of pet daycare as a function of the market price p for accommodating a pet? (Specify only the range of prices where the quantity supplied is positive)  

	•	Suppose that the demand for pet daycare is given by  What is the (short-run) equilibrium price and quantity? Is it profitable to be a supplier in this industry? What is the profit (if any)? 

	•	Knowing that entry is profitable, many others will join the business of pet daycare. What is the long-run equilibrium price? What about quantity?  

	•	Being in a competitive industry is not ideal. Suppliers always like to have some market power. One way is to create product differentiation. the Pooch hotel, with business locations in Boston ,MA; Chicago, IL; Norwalk, CT; Denver, CO; and Sunnyvale, CA, offers high-quality pet-care services, including presidential suites, webcams for 24/7 monitoring; pet spa; and personal playtime. This captures a new market for high-quality pet daycare. Suppose that the cost function is  
 What is the optimal monopoly price for the Pooch hotel ? What is the profit? Do you expect this market to become competitive in the long run? Why and why not?

Problem 3. Hair salon versioning. A local hair salon in New Haven is re-designing its business plan. The owner has discovered that they can make more money by offering two packages: “Deluxe” and “Basic”. There are two types of customers: Type A customers usually only need to get haircuts, while Type B customers would enjoy other services (wash, conditioning, coloring, ironing, etc.). Suppose that there are 4 type A customers for each type B customer. The reservations values for each type and marginal costs for each version are as follows:
Reservation Values:
Type A (4)
Type B (1)
Basic (MC=20)
$30
$60
Deluxe (MC=50)
$60
$90

The optimal menu has the form of: Get the basic package for  dollars, or get the deluxe package for  dollars. 
	•	What are the constraints that will bind under this menu?

	•	The “willing to buy” constraint: Type A pays their reservation price.
	•	The “buy the right one” constraint: Type B should prefer Deluxe. 
	•	Use the answers in part a), find the prices  and .
	•	Buy the right one constraint  Type A should buy the basic plan and pay , type B should buy the deluxe plan and pay .
	•	Willing to buy constraint 
	•	Buy the right on constraint  (or 59.99)

	•	What is the hair salon’s profit by offering this menu?  
	•	What is the hair salon’s profit if it only serves type B by offering the deluxe package only?

Optimal price for deluxe price only: 90. Profit= (90-50)*1=40.

Problem 3. Price competition and capacity [4 points]

There are two competing driving schools A and H in a town. Each can serve at most 80 students every week, and suppose each student takes a 1-hour training every week. The (weekly) cost of serving each student is $10. The two driving schools compete in price. Each can choose a hourly rate $50 or $40. Suppose each driving school aims to maximize their OWN weekly profit, and they set their prices simultaneously.

The following is the demand matrix which shows how many students would like to choose each driving school for all possible price combinations (before considering the capacity constraint). (b) Suppose now school H expands its capacity to 110. Suppose doing so does not involve any fixed cost and also does not affect the marginal cost. Draw a new profit matrix by yourself. (Again you DON’T need to upload the matrix on Canvas.) In the new Nash equilibrium, how much will school H’s profit change due to its capacity expansion? Should have school H expanded its capacity? Now H has a dominant strategy $40, and so both schools will charge $40 in NE. As a result, H’s profit will drop 300 from 3000 to 2700. [0.5 points]
Therefore, school H shouldn’t have expanded its capacity. [0.5 points] 

(c) Suppose now that school H also becomes a price leader after the capacity expansion, so that it sets its price first before school A moves.  (Hint: It will be helpful to draw a game tree by yourself which you DON’T need to upload.)

(c-1): Then what prices will the two schools charge? [1 point]

If H becomes the price leader after its capacity expansion, then the pricing game will become a sequential one. Backward induction predicts that H will charge $50, and A will follow and charge the same price.

(c-2): In this case, should school H have expanded its capacity (if doing so involves a small cost)? [0.5 points]

No. The profit outcome is now the same as before expansion, but expanding capacity is now costly.
problem_text:
Problem 1. Drones [6 points]


A risk-neutral consumer is in the market for a drone to fly around town taking video. The consumer’s value for the drone is $1000 if it is operative, and $0 if it fails. Producer A makes drones with a 5% chance of failure.

(a) (1 points).	What is the consumer’s willingness to pay for the drone?

It costs Producer A $800 to make the drone, and they price it exactly at the consumer’s willingness to pay that you found above. Everything is hunky dory until a knockoff drone supplier, Producer B, realizes they can enter the market and make money by selling an inferior product that costs $400 to make, but that has a 50% failure rate, which is indistinguishable to the consumer from the more reliable drone.
(b) (2 points).	If this new entrant were to provide as many drones as the other supplier (i.e. Producer A produces 50% of the market and Producer B produces 50% of the market so that the chance of getting a drone from either company is 50%), what is the consumer’s willingness to pay for a random drone they purchase?

 (c) (1 points).	Suppose in the short-run, the price is set exactly equal to what you found in part b. 
What is the short-term profit per drone of Producer B?

(d) (2 points).	Given what you found in (c) and (d), what will be the equilibrium price for drones, and the market share of the two companies?

(e) (1 points).	Producer A can hire an independent testing agency to show that their drones are indeed better than those from Producer B, and only have a 5% failure rate. The consumers will believe the certification of this tester. Assuming there are 1 million potential customers for drones each year, and Producer A would get back 50% of the market with the new certification, how much is Producer A willing to pay for this service annually?

given_solution:
Problem 1. Boycotts 
Note: This problem has been a tradition at Yale SOM for many years. It is a rite of passage for SOM economics students. To answer this problem requires no mathematics beyond arithmetic. Read the instructions carefully, and then feel free to talk about this problem with your fellow students. We advise such discussions to prevent you from wasting time. In the end you must see the answer yourself in your own “aha” moment and of course, the written answer must be your own. 
Setup A frequently used instrument of economic coercion is the boycott. For example, a union involved in a serious and protracted conflict with some employer or group of employers may urge all of its sympathizers to stop buying the products of that employer or group, in the hope that the resulting economic losses will provide an incentive for the employers to make concessions. However, experience shows that the coercive power of a boycott is often small. 
In this exercise, you are to use supply and demand analysis as a tool to understand the effects of boycotts. For the sake of concreteness and precision, most of the questions are posed in quantitative terms. However, the goal of the exercise is to develop your understanding of the logic of the market processes involved. Accordingly, if you have difficulty, don't try to overwhelm the problem with calculation -- rethink the economics. 
Throughout the exercise, the following assumptions are to be maintained. First, the market in question is one to which simple supply and demand analysis is applicable: there are numerous buyers and sellers in the market; the good is homogeneous; buyers and sellers seek only to trade on the most advantageous possible terms and, aside from the complications introduced by the boycott, do not care with whom they transact. Second, the identities of buyers and sellers are fixed: in particular, no reselling of the goods takes place. Third, the set of buyers divides sharply into observers and nonobservers of the boycott, while the sellers separate equally sharply into those that are targets of the boycott and those that are not. Boycott observers will not, under any circumstances, buy from a target seller, while non-observers do not care from whom they buy. For simplicity, it is assumed that boycott observers account for the same fraction of demand at every price. Similarly, target sellers account for a certain fraction of the supply at every price. 
Assume initially that in the pre-boycott situation, the market demand curve is described by: 

where  = quantity demanded and P = the price. The market supply curve has equation (with  = quantity supplied): 

except that, at prices of 5 or less, quantity supplied is zero. 
Warmup problem (i): Determine the market equilibrium in the pre-boycott situation. 
Answer: In equilibrium, the price is such that quantity demanded equals quantity supplied: 

Warmup problem (ii): Suppose that buyers representing 70% of the original demand, observe the boycott. If all non-observers buy from target sellers, what is the demand curve facing target sellers? 
Answer: Since observers will not buy from target sellers at any price, the only demand facing target sellers is from non-observers. Since observers and non-observers account for demand in the ratio 0.7:0.3 at every price, the equation of the demand curve facing target sellers is: 


Questions
Consider the three situations characterized by the following table

Observers as Fraction	Target as Fraction
Situation		of Original Demand		of Original Supply 

   A				3/4				 3/4

   B				1/4				 1/4

   C				1/2				 1/4

For each of the three situations, determine the market equilibrium that would arise under boycott conditions.  Give the quantities transacted by observing and non-observing buyers, and by target and non-target sellers.

Drawing on your results, state the general principle which relates the effectiveness of a boycott to the observance fraction and the target fraction.  “Effectiveness,” in this context, means lowering the price to target sellers.  Can you provide some intuition as well?

Hint: Warmup problem (i) supplies all the mathematical knowledge you will need to solve the remaining problems.  What is needed for the rest is an ability to structure the problem, and an understanding of simple economic principles.  The point of the exercise is to determine whether the boycott is effective in any of the three cases above.  To answer this question, you have been given the following information: (1) the market supply curve, (2) the targeted fraction of sellers, (3) the market demand curve, (4) the observers as a fraction of buyers.  Items (1) and (2) imply that it is possible to write down algebraic expressions for the supply curves of targeted and non-targeted sellers.  You can also choose to use Excel to get a sense of how prices get determined in each sub-market.

See PowerPoint for solution.  0.5 pt for getting market-clearing prices for each submarket. 0.5pt for noticing that non-observers will deviate. 1pt for noticing that only situation C “works”. 0.5pt for the general rule.

Problem 2. Gas price

Gas prices have become remarkably high since the beginning of the year 2022. On June 9th, 2022, a gallon of gasoline costs 4.06 dollars---almost 400% higher than it was on the same date of 2020 ($1.1 per gallon). We will apply what we learned about demand and supply to take a look at gas prices. 

Suppose that in 2020, the worldwide daily supply function of gasoline is given by 


The daily demand of gasoline in 2020 is:


where p is the price per gallon of gasoline and q is in the unit of million gallons.

	•	Solve for the market equilibrium for daily gasoline in 2020. What is the equilibrium price? What is the equilibrium quantity?  
	•	In the summer of 2022, people started to return to offices and to traveling. This leads to an outward shift of the demand. Suppose now that the demand becomes: 
                                                                        
In the meantime, the supply-chain problems and the Ukrainian war has led to an inward shift of the supply. Suppose the supply becomes:  What is the new equilibrium price and what is the new equilibrium quantity?

	•	What is the demand elasticity at the equilibrium of 2022? What is the supply elasticity? Which one is more elastic? 




	•	High gas prices have imposed a great burden on consumers. Suppose that the government now wishes to protect consumers by taxing gasoline supply and considers to impose a $0.5 tax per gallon on gas suppliers. What is the equilibrium price and quantity after tax?


	•	Calculate the burden of this proposed tax; what percent of the tax would be “paid” by consumers (Round to nearest percent)? How is this related to your answer in part c)?
Price increase of $0.33 out of $0.5 tax, == 67%. From part c), the demand curve is less elastic than the supply curve at the equilibrium so consumers have more tax burden.


	•	What is the amount of deadweight loss created by this policy? Is this policy a good idea?

Deadweight loss=0.5*0.5*16.67=4.16.
Taxing gas suppliers may not be a good idea consumers end up paying more. Even if the government rebates all tax revenue, there is still a deadweight loss of $4.16M

Problem 2. Eggs

“I’ve been to Trader Joe’s and Target near my apartment frequently during the past month, but I never saw a box of eggs in both of the places. Eventually, I found two dozen eggs in H Mart and spent $26 in getting them. That’s crazy,” said Lisa Li, a shopper at H Mart, one of  Seattle’s Korean grocery stores.

In 2023, nationwide egg prices have gone up by nearly 60% compared to 2022. We will use a simple demand-supply model to analyze the forces behind the price surge. 

Suppose that in 2022, the worldwide daily supply function of eggs was 


The daily demand for eggs in 2022 was:


where p is the price per box of eggs and q is in the unit of thousand boxes.

Suppose now that the demand becomes:

                                                                       

In the meantime, the avian flu has been spreading across the country. Chicken farmers have to kill all chickens that have come in close contact with some with the avian flu. As a result, supply of chickens (and hence, eggs) dramatically decreases.  Suppose the supply becomes:

Problem 3. Apple iOS

Apple is a monopolist in the market for operating systems. Its inverse demand for the new operating system is given by 

p(y) = 100 – y

Assume its marginal costs are zero for simplicity.

	•	(0.5 points) Suppose that Apple can perfectly price discriminate among the consumers. What is Apple’s profit if in that case? What is consumer surplus? Is the allocation efficient?

If Apple is able to perfectly price discriminate, it will capture all the surplus of the transaction as profits and there will be no deadweight loss. Assuming marginal costs equal to zero, profits will be equal to the entire area below the demand curve: (100) x (100) x 0.5 = 5000. Consumer surplus will be zero. The allocation will be efficient because there is no deadweight loss.  

Apple profit: (100) x (100) x 0.5 = 5000
Consumer surplus: 0
Efficient/inefficient allocation (and why): efficient because no deadweight loss


Let's get more realistic and assume that Apple can charge different prices on two segments of the market: individual buyers and firms. The inverse demands on the two segments are: 

Yi (pi ) = 50 − (4/5)pi
yf (pf) = 50 − (1/5)pf

	•	(0.5 points) If Apple does not price discriminate, what is the aggregate inverse demand?

To get the aggregate inverse demand, the first step is to identify for what price range Apple will sell only to firms (the more inelastic consumers) and for what price range it will serve both firms and individuals. The two demand functions are 
pi  = 62.5 − (5/4) Yi (pi )
pf = 250 – 5 yf (pf)

above a price of 62.5, Apple will only serve to firms and its inverse demand function will be:
Y(p) = 50 − (1/5)p

Below 62.5, it will serve both firms and individual consumers. The aggregate demand will be: 

Y(p) = Yi (p ) + Yf (p ) = 100 – p.

With this aggregate inverse demand, marginal revenues will be MR(y) = 100 – 2y; since MC = 0, y = 50 and p = 50.


 
	•	(1 point) Find the level of sales, price, and profit in this scenario (no price discrimination). 

If Apple only sells to firms, its demand is p(y) = 250 – 5y and marginal revenues are MR(y) = 250 -10y. Since MC = 0, y = 25 and p = 125. Profits will be 3,125.

If Apple sells to both individuals and firms, its demand is p(y) = 100 – y and marginal revenues are MR(y) = 100 -2y. Since MC = 0, y = 50 and p = 50. Profits will be 2,500. This profit level is lower than what Apple would get by only selling to firms, so it will choose to set p = 125, sell y = 25 only to firms, and obtain a profit of 3,125.

Sales: 25
Price: 125
Profit: 3,125

	•	(1.5 points) Compare producer's and consumer's surplus in the three cases: uniform price, perfect discrimination, and 3rd-degree price discrimination.

Uniform price: With a demand of p = 100 – y, MR(y) = 100 – 2y, y = 50, and p = 50. Profits (which are equivalent to the producer surplus) are equal to 2,500. Consumer surplus is the triangle between the price level (50) and the demand curve, and can be calculated as (100-50) [the height of the triangle] x 50 [the quantity] x 0.5 = 1,250.
Perfect discrimination: Producer surplus is 5,000, consumer surplus is 0 (see point 1).
3rd degree price discrimination: 
Apple will cater to each individual market segment separately, choosing different prices and quantities. We already know from point 3 that it will charge firms a price p=125, sell y = 25, and make a profit/producer surplus of 3,125. Firms (as consumers) will get a consumer surplus of (250-125) x 25 x 0.5=1562.5. 
The demand function for individual consumers is p(y) = 62.5 – (5/4)y, which implies MR(y) = 62.5 – (5/2)y. The optimal quantity is y = 25, the optimal price is p = 31.25, and profits are 781.25. Consumer surplus is (62.5-31.25) x 25 x 0.5 = 390.625. So as a whole, under 3rd degree price discrimination, profits are equal to 3,125 + 781.25 = 3,906.25, consumer surplus is 390.625 + 1562.5 = 1,953.125.

Problem 4 (13 points) 

Tesla offers two versions of its Model S sedan, the standard version and an “extended range” version. However, the two cars are physically identical; the standard version’s software disables some of the onboard batteries to limit the range (this is a classic “damaged goods” strategy). So, the cost to manufacture either version is the same, approximately $50,000 per car. 

After conducting some market research, they have identified two segments of car buyers: Luxury buyers, who have high reservation prices for a Tesla, and Eco buyers, who are more price sensitive and are considering competing electric vehicles as well. Problem 5 (19 points) 

A Yale undergrad has come to you for advice. They are graduating and looking at job options and have been offered two jobs, one that has a guaranteed salary of $85,000 per year, and the other that has a lower salary of $60,000 per year but has a 75% chance of also having a $40,000 bonus. 

This student is risk-averse and so is considering both opportunities. Their utility function is represented by where x is measured in thousands.  They have no other savings or wealth. 

	•	(2 points) What is the expected value of the job with the bonus? 

Answer: $90K


	•	(5 points) Which job do you expect them to take? 

Expected utility is 11.35 with the first job and 11.38 with the second. They will choose the second.

	•	(6 points) What is the minimum guaranteed salary the first job would have to offer to have this student prefer it over the risker job? 

The question is asking for the certainty equivalent (CE) of the risky job, defined as ln(CE)= 11.38. The CE is thus approximately $88K.

	•	(6 points) Suppose you were instead told that the student is risk-seeking. Would your answers to points a) and c) above be higher/the same/lower than previously found? Would your answer to point b) above change?   Explain.

If the student is risk seeking he will like the risky job even more, so the answer in question b) would be the same. The expected value of the job with the bonus would not change because it does not depend on the utility function, so the answer to a) remains unchanged. The CE would change and it would be lower.

GAME THEORY

given_solution:
Problem 6: Extended location games [25 points]
Recall the location choice game discussed in the class: Tourists are evenly distributed on a beach that is one mile long. Two ice cream vendors simultaneously choose where to locate, each aiming to maximize their own market share. (Suppose they can locate exactly at the same spot if they want.) Tourists prefer to buy from the closer vendor. When they are indifferent between vendors, they randomly choose one. In the following, we consider a few variants of this location choice game.
Suppose that there are now THREE ice cream vendors.
(a) [5 points] Is it a Nash equilibrium that they all locate in the middle of the beach? Please explain your answer.
No, it is not a NE. 
When they all locate in the middle, each vendor has a market share 1/3. But if a vendor individually deviates from the middles slightly, its market share can be close to 1/2. 
(b) [5 points] Is it a Nash equilibrium that they locate at 1/6, 1/2, and 5/6, respectively (so that each has a market share 1/3)? Please explain your answer.
No, it is not a NE. 
The vendor at 1/6 or 5/6 has a unilateral incentive to move toward the vendor in the middle. (The vendor at the middle actually has NO strict incentive to move.) 
(c) [5 points] Suppose now we “bend” the beach so that it becomes a CIRCLE that has a circumference of one mile (i.e., the two endpoints 0 and 1 are now connected to each other). Is the location configuration in question (b) a Nash equilibrium? Please explain your answer.
Yes, it is now a NE. 
Given the other two vendors’ locations, a vendor’s market share cannot exceed 1/3 no matter where it moves to. 
Let us now return to the original location game with only TWO ice cream vendors and a LINEAR beach.
(d) [5 points] Suppose now each tourist never travels more than 1/4 mile to buy an ice cream. That is, if the distance to either vendor is longer than 1/4 mile, a tourist will not buy. What are the Nash equilibria now?
The only Nash equilibrium is that one vendor at 1/4 and the other at 3/4.
(e) [5 points] Suppose now each tourist never travels more than 1/5 mile to buy an ice cream. How will your answer change?
One Nash equilibrium is that one vendor at 1/5 and the other at 4/5. But now there are many other Nash equilibria. For example, one at 1/4 and the other at 3/4. In fact, any configuration is a NE where the two vendors are at least 2/5 mile away from each other and their distance to the closer endpoint is at least 1/5 mile.

Problem 7: Investment game [20 points]
Consider the following sequential-move game: firm 1 first decides whether to adopt an aggressive low-price policy or an accommodating high-price policy; after seeing firm 1’s choice, firm 2 decides whether or not to invest in a new technology which reduces its production cost. The investment cost is k.
If firm 1 sets a low price and firm 2 doesn’t invest, then firm 1 will earn 5, and firm 2 will have no business (e.g., because its production cost is too high) and earn 0. If firm 1 sets a low price and firm 2 invests to reduce its production cost, then firm 1 earns 1 and firm 2 earns 4 (before considering its investment cost). If firm 1 sets a high price and firm 2 doesn’t invest, firm 1 will earn 4, and firm 2 will have some business and earn 2. If firm 1 sets a high price and firm 2 invests, then firm 1 will have no business and earn 0, and firm 2 will earn 5 (before considering its investment cost).
(a) [5 points] Draw the game tree of this sequential-move game. [Hint: payoffs are net profit, so don’t forget the investment cost.]


(b) [10 points] For what values of k, will firm 1 choose to set a high price in the beginning?
k should be between 3 and 4.
Two conditions should be satisfied for firm 1 to set a high price: First, when firm 1 sets a high price, firm 2 should respond by choosing not to invest. (Otherwise, firm 1 would get 0, which must be worse than choosing a low price.) This requires 5-k<2, or k>3. Second, when firm 1 sets a low price, firm 2 should respond by choosing to invest. (Otherwise, firm 1 would get 5, in which case it wouldn’t choose a high price.) This requires 4-k>0, or k<4.
(c) [5 points] Suppose the investment cost increases from k=2.5 to k=3.5. Will firm 2 suffer or benefit from that? Please explain your answer.
Firm 2 benefits from the increase of k. 
When k=2.5, firm 1 will set a low price, and then firm 2 will invest, in which case firm 2 gets net profit 1.5. When k=3.5, firm 1 will set a high price, and then firm 2 will not invest, in which case firm 2 gets net profit 2. (A higher investment cost induces firm 1 to be less aggressive in the beginning.)

Consider the following sequential-move game: firm 1 first decides whether to adopt an aggressive low-price policy or an accommodating high-price policy; after seeing firm 1’s choice, firm 2 decides whether or not to invest in a new technology which reduces its production cost. If firm 1 sets a low price and firm 2 doesn’t invest, then firm 1 will earn 5, and firm 2 will have no business (e.g., because its production cost is too high) and earn 0. If firm 1 sets a high price and firm 2 doesn’t invest, firm 1 will earn 4, and firm 2 will have some business and earn 2. If firm 1 sets a high price and firm 2 invests, then firm 1 will have no business and earn 0, and firm 2 will earn 5 (before considering its investment cost).

k should be between 3 and 4.

(Otherwise, firm 1 would get 0, which must be worse than choosing a low price.) This requires 5-k<2, or k>3. Second, when firm 1 sets a low price, firm 2 should respond by choosing to invest. (Otherwise, firm 1 would get 5, in which case it wouldn’t choose a high price.) This requires 4-k>0, or k<4.

(A higher investment cost induces firm 1 to be less aggressive in the beginning.)

Problem 1: Electric Vehicles (18 points)

Electric Vehicles (EVs) have lower carbon footprints than gasoline-powered cars and do not produce as much fine particle matter pollution as combustion from gasoline/diesel car engines do. However, these environmental benefits are not directly taken into account when purchasing EV’s.  In other words, EVs have positive externality. 

	•	(2 points) Without any market interventions, will EV consumption be lower or higher relative to the efficient level? 
Under-consumption due to positive externality. 


Suppose that, in the city of New Haven, the demand of gasoline-powered cars is given by 


Meanwhile, suppose that the supply of EVs is given by 


	•	(4 points) What is the equilibrium price and quantity of gas-powered cars in New Haven (without government intervention)? What are the demand and supply elasticities at this equilibrium? Which side is more elastic?   40-p=4p => 5p=40, p=8, q=32.  Demand elasticity: p/q*(-1)=8/32*(-1)=-0.25 Supply elasticity: p/q*4=8/32*4=1  Supply is more elastic.  

The city of New Haven decided to intervene the market and encourage consumers to switch from gas-powered cars to EVs. Suppose that the city imposes a per-unit tax in the amount of $10. 
 
	•	(4 points) What is the equilibrium price and quantity of gas-powered cars in New Haven after tax? What percentage of tax burdens do consumers bear.   New demand: 
               New equilibrium price producers get:                                New equilibrium price consumers pay: 6+10=16                                New equilibrium quantity: 4*6=24                 Consumers bear (16-8)/10=80%
 
	•	(3 points) What is the amount of deadweight loss in the gas-powered car market?  Deadweight loss= ½*10*(32-24)=40. 
 
	•	(3 points) Suppose that after tax, all the gas-powered car consumers with reservation prices lower than the new equilibrium price switch to EVs. How many more EVs are purchased after tax?   32-24=8 units 
	•	(2 points) Overall, what can you say about this policy?   Taxing gas-powered cars reduces consumption of gas-powered cars and increases consumption of EVs, which could be beneficial as it helps with the under-production problem. However, (i) it creates deadweight loss in the gas-powered car market and (ii) it might lead to over-consumption of EV if the tax is too high.

given_solution:
Problem 2: Gravel market (25 points)

Quarry Inc. has a local monopoly over the gravel industry in central Connecticut. The inverse demand for gravel is given by Q =100-2P, where Q is the quantity of gravel in thousand cubic yards per month, and P is the price in dollars per cubic yard. Quarry Inc. incurs a fixed cost of production of $12 and a variable cost of $5 per cubic yard. 

	•	(4 points) What are the profit maximizing price and quantity choices for Quarry Inc.?

P=50-0.5Q 
Revenue=P x Q = 50Q-0.5Q2
MR=50-Q
So MR=MC gives 50-Q=5 or Q=45 and P=27.5

	•	(4 points) What is the elasticity of demand at this price? Does this elasticity make sense to you? Why or why not?
 

The elasticity can be derived using the Inverse Elasticity Pricing Rule, which states that (P-MC)/P=-1/elasticity. This implies that  Elasticity = P/(MC-P)= 27.5/(5-27.5)=-1.22. 


	•	(2 points) What is the total profit of Quarry Inc. at the optimal price and quantity choice?

Profit = P * Q – TC(Q) = (27.5 * 45) – (5*45+12)=1000.5 


	•	(3 points) If the market were instead perfectly competitive, with many gravel providers (all with identical marginal costs as the monopolist), what would you predict the price of gravel to be? What would be the quantity sold?

In competitive markets, price equals marginal cost, so P=5.
Quantity can be derived using the inverse demand function: Q = 100 – 2*5=90.

	•	(3 points) What would the firm’s profit be in this scenario? What would the firm do in the long vs short run?

In the short run, the firm’s profits would be negative and equal to -12l the firm would still produce to cover the fixed costs. In the long run, the firm would exit.

	•	(4 points) Let’s go back to the setting with Quarry Inc. being a monopoly (i.e., part (d) no longer applies.) The CT state government believes that local builders are suffering because of Quarry Inc.’s monopoly power and consequent high prices. To extract some surplus from the monopoly, the government levies a $1 tax (to be paid by Quarry Inc.) for every cubic yard of gravel sold. Compute the new price and quantity that you expect in the market.

The tax will be like increasing the per unit cost of the monopolist by 1, so MC goes up by 1.
To find profit maximizing price and quantity, we can set MR = MC like in point (a), which yields 50-Q=6. This gives Q=44 and P = 28.

	
	•	(5 points) Do you think the above tax is effective in helping the local builders? Explain quantitatively why or why not?

Not effective.
Price is now higher for consumer and less quantity being sold. So consumers are now worse off.	
Earlier (point a), the CS of the local builders was 0.5*(50-27.5)*45=506.25
New CS = 0.5*(50-28)*44 =484. This implies that CS went down by 22.25.

Problem 3: Job offers (20 points) 

Knowing you have taken Basics of Economics, a non-MBA friend who is deciding between job options has come to you for advice. They have been offered two jobs, one that has a guaranteed salary of $120,000 per year, and the other that has a lower salary of $100,000 per year but has a 75% chance of also having a $60,000 bonus. 

This student is risk-averse and so is considering both opportunities. Their utility function is represented by  where x is measured in thousands so that . They have no other savings or wealth. 

	•	(2 points) What is the expected value of the two jobs? 

Job with no bonus: 120K wp1, EV = 120K.
Job with bonus: 100K wp 0.25, 160K wp 0.75. EV = 0.25 * 100K + 0.75 * 160K = 145K.

	•	(4 points) Which job do you expect them to take? 

Utility of job with no bonus is ; Expected utility of job with bonus is . Friend is better off in the job with a bonus.

	•	(2 points) Would your answer change if your friend told you they are risk-neutral? Why or why not?

If friend is risk neutral, they will care about expected value. EV of bonus job is greater than EV of fixed wage job, so they will still choose the job with the bonus.

	•	(6 points) What is the minimum guaranteed salary the first job would have to offer to make the student indifferent between the two jobs?

Call this salary S; we need , which implies that S = 143,684. Partial credit (3 points) if the explain concept of certainty equivalent but don’t write down formula.


	•	(6 points) The firm offering the bonus has decided to offer a different contract for new analysts to accept. This new contract maintains the Expected Value of the existing contract but puts more weight on the bonus. Is this a good idea or bad idea when hiring potentially risk-averse employees, and why? 

Answer: bad to go more high-powered if people are risk averse. Full credit if they note that putting more of the salary in the bonus exposes the individual to more risk, and that this is costly to do if they are risk averse. Give 3-4 points for anything reasonable.

Problem 4: An extended HFT investment game (25 points)
Consider an extended version of the high-frequency-trading game discussed in the class. Suppose now that when a firm invests, it can choose whether to do a high investment (HI) or a low investment (LI). The matrix form of this extended game is as follows, where “No” means no investment and X and Y are unknown numbers:

                                    Firm 2
Firm 1

HI
LI
No

HI
30, 30
X, 25
60, 20

LI
25, X
40, 40
Y, 35

No
20, 60
35, Y
50, 50

	•	[8 points] For what values of X and Y, will HI be a dominant strategy for each firm?
HI is a dominant strategy if X>40 [4 points] and Y<60 [4 points] (or if X>=40 and Y=<60).
	•	[8 points] For what values of X and Y, will this game have more than one Nash equilibrium?
First notice that (HI, HI) is always a NE, and other outcomes cannot be a NE except for (LI, LI). (This can be seen by using best-response method.) 
For (LI, LI) to be another NE, we need X=<40 [4 points] (or X<40), but the value of Y does not matter, so any Y will work [4 points].
	•	[9 points] For what values of X and Y, will firm 1 earn 35 in the sequential-move version of this game where firm 1 moves first? 
By backward induction, for firm 1 to earn 35 we need two conditions: (i) firm 1 is willing to choose No; and then (ii) firm 2 is willing to choose LI afterwards. The latter requires Y>60 [4 points], and the former requires X<40 [5 points] (otherwise firm 2 would choose LI after firm 1 chooses LI, in which case firm 1 would earn 40 so that it wouldn’t choose No in the beginning). (Y>=60 and X=<40 are also fine.)

given_solution:
Problem 5: An extended location choice game (20 points)
Consider an extended version of the location choice game discussed in the class. Tourists are evenly distributed on a beach that is a line segment [0,1] of one mile long. THREE ice cream vendors simultaneously choose where to locate, each aiming to maximize their own market share. (Suppose they can locate exactly at the same spot if they want.) Tourists prefer to buy from the closer vendor. When they are indifferent between multiple vendors, they randomly choose one. 
	•	[5 points] Is it a Nash equilibrium that the three vendors all locate at the middle of the beach (i.e., at 1/2)? Please explain your answer.
No, it is not a NE. [3 points]
When they all locate in the middle, each vendor has a market share 1/3. But if a vendor individually deviates from the middle slightly, its market share can be close to 1/2. [2 points for pointing out that any vendor will have an incentive to deviate.]
	•	[5 points] Is it a Nash equilibrium that the three vendors locate at 1/6, 1/2, and 5/6, respectively (so that each has a market share 1/3)? Please explain your answer.
No, it is not a NE. [3 points]
The vendor at 1/6 or 5/6 has a unilateral incentive to move toward the vendor in the middle. [2 points] (The vendor at the middle actually has NO strict incentive to move.)  [only 1 point if it says ANY vendor has an individual incentive to deviate.]
	•	[5 points] Suppose now we “bend” the beach so that it becomes a CIRCLE that has a circumference of one mile (i.e., the two endpoints 0 and 1 are now connected to each other). Is the location configuration in question (b) a Nash equilibrium? Please explain your answer.
Yes, it is now a NE. [3 points]
Given the other two vendors’ locations, a vendor’s market share cannot exceed 1/3 no matter where it moves to. [2 points]
	•	[5 points] Return to the original case where the beach is a line segment. Suppose that vendor 1 has already entered the market and located at the left endpoint 0. Now vendors 2 and 3 simultaneously choose where to locate. What is the Nah equilibrium of the location choice game between vendors 2 and 3?
Both vendors 2 and 3 will locate at 2/3 in the NE. [5 points; no explanation needed.] It is easy to verify that none of them has an individual incentive to move away from this location.
(Here is a way to show this is the unique NE. It is clearly not a NE if vendors 2 and 3 are not in the same location. Suppose now they are at the same location, say, x. Obviously it is not a NE if x=0. Suppose then x>0. In that case, they will each have a market share 0.5(1-0.5x). If one of them, say, vendor 2 moves slightly to the right of vendor 3, it will have a market share almost equal to 1-x; if vendor 2 moves to the left of vendor 3, it will have a market share of 0.5x. In a NE, neither of the deviation should be profitable. You can easily check that this requires x=2/3.)

Problem 1: Electric Vehicles (18 points)

Electric Vehicles (EVs) have lower carbon footprints than gasoline-powered cars and do not produce as much fine particle matter pollution as combustion from gasoline/diesel car engines do. However, these environmental benefits are not directly taken into account when purchasing EV’s.  In other words, EVs have positive externality. 

	•	(2 points) Without any market interventions, will EV consumption be lower or higher relative to the efficient level? 
Under-consumption due to positive externality. 


Suppose that, in the city of New Haven, the demand of gasoline-powered cars is given by 


Meanwhile, suppose that the supply of EVs is given by 


	•	(4 points) What is the equilibrium price and quantity of gas-powered cars in New Haven (without government intervention)? What are the demand and supply elasticities at this equilibrium? Which side is more elastic?   40-p=4p => 5p=40, p=8, q=32.  Demand elasticity: p/q*(-1)=8/32*(-1)=-0.25 Supply elasticity: p/q*4=8/32*4=1  Supply is more elastic.  

The city of New Haven decided to intervene the market and encourage consumers to switch from gas-powered cars to EVs. Suppose that the city imposes a per-unit tax in the amount of $10. 
 
	•	(4 points) What is the equilibrium price and quantity of gas-powered cars in New Haven after tax? What percentage of tax burdens do consumers bear.   New demand: 
               New equilibrium price producers get:                                New equilibrium price consumers pay: 6+10=16                                New equilibrium quantity: 4*6=24                 Consumers bear (16-8)/10=80%
 
	•	(3 points) What is the amount of deadweight loss in the gas-powered car market?  Deadweight loss= ½*10*(32-24)=40. 
 
	•	(3 points) Suppose that after tax, all the gas-powered car consumers with reservation prices lower than the new equilibrium price switch to EVs. How many more EVs are purchased after tax?   32-24=8 units 
	•	(2 points) Overall, what can you say about this policy?   Taxing gas-powered cars reduces consumption of gas-powered cars and increases consumption of EVs, which could be beneficial as it helps with the under-production problem. However, (i) it creates deadweight loss in the gas-powered car market and (ii) it might lead to over-consumption of EV if the tax is too high.

given_solution:
Problem 2: Gravel market (25 points)

Quarry Inc. has a local monopoly over the gravel industry in central Connecticut. The inverse demand for gravel is given by Q =100-2P, where Q is the quantity of gravel in thousand cubic yards per month, and P is the price in dollars per cubic yard. Quarry Inc. incurs a fixed cost of production of $12 and a variable cost of $5 per cubic yard. 

	•	(4 points) What are the profit maximizing price and quantity choices for Quarry Inc.?

P=50-0.5Q 
Revenue=P x Q = 50Q-0.5Q2
MR=50-Q
So MR=MC gives 50-Q=5 or Q=45 and P=27.5

	•	(4 points) What is the elasticity of demand at this price? Does this elasticity make sense to you? Why or why not?
 

The elasticity can be derived using the Inverse Elasticity Pricing Rule, which states that (P-MC)/P=-1/elasticity. This implies that  Elasticity = P/(MC-P)= 27.5/(5-27.5)=-1.22. 


	•	(2 points) What is the total profit of Quarry Inc. at the optimal price and quantity choice?

Profit = P * Q – TC(Q) = (27.5 * 45) – (5*45+12)=1000.5 


	•	(3 points) If the market were instead perfectly competitive, with many gravel providers (all with identical marginal costs as the monopolist), what would you predict the price of gravel to be? What would be the quantity sold?

In competitive markets, price equals marginal cost, so P=5.
Quantity can be derived using the inverse demand function: Q = 100 – 2*5=90.

	•	(3 points) What would the firm’s profit be in this scenario? What would the firm do in the long vs short run?

In the short run, the firm’s profits would be negative and equal to -12l the firm would still produce to cover the fixed costs. In the long run, the firm would exit.

	•	(4 points) Let’s go back to the setting with Quarry Inc. being a monopoly (i.e., part (d) no longer applies.) The CT state government believes that local builders are suffering because of Quarry Inc.’s monopoly power and consequent high prices. To extract some surplus from the monopoly, the government levies a $1 tax (to be paid by Quarry Inc.) for every cubic yard of gravel sold. Compute the new price and quantity that you expect in the market.

The tax will be like increasing the per unit cost of the monopolist by 1, so MC goes up by 1.
To find profit maximizing price and quantity, we can set MR = MC like in point (a), which yields 50-Q=6. This gives Q=44 and P = 28.

	
	•	(5 points) Do you think the above tax is effective in helping the local builders? Explain quantitatively why or why not?

Not effective.
Price is now higher for consumer and less quantity being sold. So consumers are now worse off.	
Earlier (point a), the CS of the local builders was 0.5*(50-27.5)*45=506.25
New CS = 0.5*(50-28)*44 =484. This implies that CS went down by 22.25.

Which side is more elastic?   40-p=4p => 5p=40, p=8, q=32.  Demand elasticity: p/q*(-1)=8/32*(-1)=-0.25 Supply elasticity: p/q*4=8/32*4=1  Supply is more elastic.  

The city of New Haven decided to intervene the market and encourage consumers to switch from gas-powered cars to EVs. Suppose that the city imposes a per-unit tax in the amount of $10. 
 
	•	(4 points) What is the equilibrium price and quantity of gas-powered cars in New Haven after tax? What percentage of tax burdens do consumers bear.   New demand: 
               New equilibrium price producers get:                                New equilibrium price consumers pay: 6+10=16                                New equilibrium quantity: 4*6=24                 Consumers bear (16-8)/10=80%
 
	•	(3 points) What is the amount of deadweight loss in the gas-powered car market?  Deadweight loss= ½*10*(32-24)=40. 
 
	•	(3 points) Suppose that after tax, all the gas-powered car consumers with reservation prices lower than the new equilibrium price switch to EVs. How many more EVs are purchased after tax?   32-24=8 units 
	•	(2 points) Overall, what can you say about this policy?   Taxing gas-powered cars reduces consumption of gas-powered cars and increases consumption of EVs, which could be beneficial as it helps with the under-production problem. However, (i) it creates deadweight loss in the gas-powered car market and (ii) it might lead to over-consumption of EV if the tax is too high.
