Problem 1 (20 points)

From the second quarter of 2020 to the fourth quarter of 2022, the median home sales price went up by 48%. This question explores a possible explanation for the rising cost of single-family houses. 

Suppose that, in 2020, the demand for houses was:

Meanwhile, the supply of houses in 2020 was: 


	•	(3 points) What is the equilibrium price and quantity of the housing market in 2020?

In 2020, people started to spend a lot more time at home because of Covid. Therefore, many people wanted to scale up their houses. Moreover, the booming stock market gave a lot of cash to individual investors, causing them to have higher willingness to pay. This leads to an increase in demand. Suppose that the demand for houses in 2022 became: 


	•	(3 points) What would the equilibrium price and quantity be if the supply had stayed the same? 

Moreover, the supply chain problem made it difficult and more costly for new constructions to gather materials. Social distancing and other Covid prevention measures also made any in-person work in the construction process (e.g., wiring, plumbing, and application of permits etc) difficult. These caused the supply to decrease. Suppose that the supply in 2022 became:  




	•	(4 points) What is the equilibrium price and quantity in 2022? 

To make housing more affordable, suppose that the government decides to provide subsidies to home buyers. 
If there is a $1 per-unit subsidy to home buyers, what would the equilibrium price and quantity be? What is the total expenditure? What is the deadweight loss? 

	•	(5 points) If there is a $1 per-unit subsidy to home buyers, what would the equilibrium price and quantity be? What is the total expenditure? What is the deadweight loss?

	•	(5 points) Would it be better for the producers if they were subsidized $1 per unit instead of the consumers? Why or why not? Which side of the market benefits more from subsidies?

given_solution:
Problem 2: Gravel market (25 points)

Quarry Inc. has a local monopoly over the gravel industry in central Connecticut. The inverse demand for gravel is given by Q =100-2P, where Q is the quantity of gravel in thousand cubic yards per month, and P is the price in dollars per cubic yard. Quarry Inc. incurs a fixed cost of production of $12 and a variable cost of $5 per cubic yard. 

	•	(4 points) What are the profit maximizing price and quantity choices for Quarry Inc.?

P=50-0.5Q 
Revenue=P x Q = 50Q-0.5Q2
MR=50-Q
So MR=MC gives 50-Q=5 or Q=45 and P=27.5

	•	(4 points) What is the elasticity of demand at this price? Does this elasticity make sense to you? Why or why not?
 

The elasticity can be derived using the Inverse Elasticity Pricing Rule, which states that (P-MC)/P=-1/elasticity. This implies that  Elasticity = P/(MC-P)= 27.5/(5-27.5)=-1.22. 


	•	(2 points) What is the total profit of Quarry Inc. at the optimal price and quantity choice?

Profit = P * Q – TC(Q) = (27.5 * 45) – (5*45+12)=1000.5 


	•	(3 points) If the market were instead perfectly competitive, with many gravel providers (all with identical marginal costs as the monopolist), what would you predict the price of gravel to be? What would be the quantity sold?

In competitive markets, price equals marginal cost, so P=5.
Quantity can be derived using the inverse demand function: Q = 100 – 2*5=90.

	•	(3 points) What would the firm’s profit be in this scenario? What would the firm do in the long vs short run?

In the short run, the firm’s profits would be negative and equal to -12l the firm would still produce to cover the fixed costs. In the long run, the firm would exit.

	•	(4 points) Let’s go back to the setting with Quarry Inc. being a monopoly (i.e., part (d) no longer applies.) The CT state government believes that local builders are suffering because of Quarry Inc.’s monopoly power and consequent high prices. To extract some surplus from the monopoly, the government levies a $1 tax (to be paid by Quarry Inc.) for every cubic yard of gravel sold. Compute the new price and quantity that you expect in the market.

The tax will be like increasing the per unit cost of the monopolist by 1, so MC goes up by 1.
To find profit maximizing price and quantity, we can set MR = MC like in point (a), which yields 50-Q=6. This gives Q=44 and P = 28.

	
	•	(5 points) Do you think the above tax is effective in helping the local builders? Explain quantitatively why or why not?

Not effective.
Price is now higher for consumer and less quantity being sold. So consumers are now worse off.	
Earlier (point a), the CS of the local builders was 0.5*(50-27.5)*45=506.25
New CS = 0.5*(50-28)*44 =484. This implies that CS went down by 22.25.

Problem 2 (24 points) 

CalTrain has a local monopoly in the market for railway services between Palo Alto and San Francisco. The inverse demand for rides is given by P(Q) =(12-2)/25Q, where Q is the number of tickets sold and P is the price of a ride. CalTrain incurs in variable cost of $2 per train ride and has fixed costs of zero.
	•	(6 points) Calculate the profit maximizing price and quantity for Caltrain. 

	•	(6 points) What are the company’s profits? What is consumer surplus? What is the deadweight loss?

	•	(6 points) Suppose that, upon further market research, CalTrain finds out that they are serving two distinct groups of customers: Business, with an inverse demand of P(Q) = 20-0.4Q, and Students, with an inverse demand of P(Q) = 10-0.1Q. If CalTrain can price-discriminate, what would be the prices offered to the two groups of customers? What is the total number of tickets sold? What is the profit?

	•	(4 points) Suppose CalTrain is now able to engage in perfect price discrimination. Considering the total inverse demand of point (a), what would be the company’s profits and what would be consumer surplus?

Problem 3: Job offers (20 points) 

Knowing you have taken Basics of Economics, a non-MBA friend who is deciding between job options has come to you for advice. They have been offered two jobs, one that has a guaranteed salary of $120,000 per year, and the other that has a lower salary of $100,000 per year but has a 75% chance of also having a $60,000 bonus. 

This student is risk-averse and so is considering both opportunities. Their utility function is represented by  where x is measured in thousands so that . They have no other savings or wealth. 

	•	(2 points) What is the expected value of the two jobs? 

Job with no bonus: 120K wp1, EV = 120K.
Job with bonus: 100K wp 0.25, 160K wp 0.75. EV = 0.25 * 100K + 0.75 * 160K = 145K.

	•	(4 points) Which job do you expect them to take? 

Utility of job with no bonus is ; Expected utility of job with bonus is . Friend is better off in the job with a bonus.

	•	(2 points) Would your answer change if your friend told you they are risk-neutral? Why or why not?

If friend is risk neutral, they will care about expected value. EV of bonus job is greater than EV of fixed wage job, so they will still choose the job with the bonus.

	•	(6 points) What is the minimum guaranteed salary the first job would have to offer to make the student indifferent between the two jobs?

Call this salary S; we need , which implies that S = 143,684. Partial credit (3 points) if the explain concept of certainty equivalent but don’t write down formula.


	•	(6 points) The firm offering the bonus has decided to offer a different contract for new analysts to accept. This new contract maintains the Expected Value of the existing contract but puts more weight on the bonus. Is this a good idea or bad idea when hiring potentially risk-averse employees, and why? 

Answer: bad to go more high-powered if people are risk averse. Full credit if they note that putting more of the salary in the bonus exposes the individual to more risk, and that this is costly to do if they are risk averse. Give 3-4 points for anything reasonable.

Problem 3 (21 points) 

A friend is deciding between two job offers: one from firm Flat, which pays a flat wage of $95,000, and one from firm P4P, which pays a base salary of $75,000 and a bonus of $45,000 upon reaching a performance target. Suppose your friend assigns a 50% probability to reaching the performance target.

	•	(2 points) What is the expected value of the two jobs to your friend? 

	•	(2 points) If your friend is risk-neutral, what job would they pick?

	•	(3 points) Would your answer change if your friend is risk-averse, with a utility function u(x)=  ?

Suppose now that you are a HR manager at firm P4P and are deciding whether to change your compensation scheme. Namely, you are considering adjusting your base salary and bonus to incentivize employees to work hard. Suppose your employees can choose two levels of effort: “low”, which gives them a chance equal to 30% of meeting the performance target, and “high”, which gives them a chance equal to 70%. The cost of effort is equal to $30,000. Assume, as above, that your main competitor – firm Flat – is paying employees a flat wage of $95,000.

	•	(6 points) Assuming all employees are risk-neutral, find the optimal base salary and bonus you would have to pay if you wanted to implement the bonus scheme.

	•	(2 points) Would your answers to point d) change if you found out your employees where risk averse? Why?

	•	(6 points) Suppose that, upon further research, you find out that the profit margins from meeting the performance target is $60,000 per worker (that is, each worker meeting the target increases your profits, net of all non-labor costs, by $60,000). Would it be worth it for your company to stick with the bonus scheme, or would it be better off to switch to the flat wage scheme? (For this point, assume again workers are risk-neutral). Explain.

given_solution:
Problem 4: An extended HFT investment game (25 points)
Consider an extended version of the high-frequency-trading game discussed in the class. Suppose now that when a firm invests, it can choose whether to do a high investment (HI) or a low investment (LI). The matrix form of this extended game is as follows, where “No” means no investment and X and Y are unknown numbers:

                                    Firm 2
Firm 1

HI
LI
No

HI
30, 30
X, 25
60, 20

LI
25, X
40, 40
Y, 35

No
20, 60
35, Y
50, 50

	•	[8 points] For what values of X and Y, will HI be a dominant strategy for each firm?
HI is a dominant strategy if X>40 [4 points] and Y<60 [4 points] (or if X>=40 and Y=<60).
	•	[8 points] For what values of X and Y, will this game have more than one Nash equilibrium?
First notice that (HI, HI) is always a NE, and other outcomes cannot be a NE except for (LI, LI). (This can be seen by using best-response method.) 
For (LI, LI) to be another NE, we need X=<40 [4 points] (or X<40), but the value of Y does not matter, so any Y will work [4 points].
	•	[9 points] For what values of X and Y, will firm 1 earn 35 in the sequential-move version of this game where firm 1 moves first? 
By backward induction, for firm 1 to earn 35 we need two conditions: (i) firm 1 is willing to choose No; and then (ii) firm 2 is willing to choose LI afterwards. The latter requires Y>60 [4 points], and the former requires X<40 [5 points] (otherwise firm 2 would choose LI after firm 1 chooses LI, in which case firm 1 would earn 40 so that it wouldn’t choose No in the beginning). (Y>=60 and X=<40 are also fine.)

Problem 4: Price Competition and Capacity [20 points]
Consider a one-shot price competition between Apple and Samsung for their new smartphones. Each company can chose to charge either $800 or $1,000. The following is the DEMAND matrix (not the profit matrix):
                                        Samsung

Apple

$800
$1000

$800
30, 20
35, 10

$1000
18, 22
21, 18

For example, if both firms set a price of $800, Apple will have a demand of 30 million units and Samsung will have a demand of 20 million units. (While the demand numbers are in Millions, you don’t need to account for that in your calculations.) Suppose the production cost per unit is $400, the same for both companies. (We don’t consider fixed costs as they usually don’t impact price competition.) Assume each company aims to maximize its own profits.
a) Suppose first that the two companies set their prices simultaneously. Please answer the following questions. [Hint: Start by constructing the game matrix with profit numbers. Don’t forget to include costs, and be sure to double check your calculations before proceeding.]
(a-1) Does Samsung have a dominant strategy? If so, please specify. [4 points]
(a-2) What prices will the two companies set in the Nash Equilibrium? [4 points]

b) Suppose now that Apple announces its price first, and then Samsung responds. Please answer the following questions. [Hint: Start by drawing the game tree.]
(b-1) What prices will the two companies set? [4 points]
(b-2) If Samsung announces its price first, how will each company’s profit change? [4 points]

c) Return to the simultaneous-move game, but now suppose Apple has a capacity constraint of 25 million units, while Samsung does not. In other words, Apple cannot serve more than 25 million customers, and any unserved customers will buy from Samsung. Please answer the following questions. [Hint: You need to re-draw a game matrix.]
(c-1) What prices will the two companies now set in the Nash Equilibrium? [2 points]
(c-2) Compared to the situation in question (a), does Apple suffer or benefit from its capacity constraint, and by how much? [2 points]

given_solution:
Problem 5: An extended location choice game (20 points)
Consider an extended version of the location choice game discussed in the class. Tourists are evenly distributed on a beach that is a line segment [0,1] of one mile long. THREE ice cream vendors simultaneously choose where to locate, each aiming to maximize their own market share. (Suppose they can locate exactly at the same spot if they want.) Tourists prefer to buy from the closer vendor. When they are indifferent between multiple vendors, they randomly choose one. 
	•	[5 points] Is it a Nash equilibrium that the three vendors all locate at the middle of the beach (i.e., at 1/2)? Please explain your answer.
No, it is not a NE. [3 points]
When they all locate in the middle, each vendor has a market share 1/3. But if a vendor individually deviates from the middle slightly, its market share can be close to 1/2. [2 points for pointing out that any vendor will have an incentive to deviate.]
	•	[5 points] Is it a Nash equilibrium that the three vendors locate at 1/6, 1/2, and 5/6, respectively (so that each has a market share 1/3)? Please explain your answer.
No, it is not a NE. [3 points]
The vendor at 1/6 or 5/6 has a unilateral incentive to move toward the vendor in the middle. [2 points] (The vendor at the middle actually has NO strict incentive to move.)  [only 1 point if it says ANY vendor has an individual incentive to deviate.]
	•	[5 points] Suppose now we “bend” the beach so that it becomes a CIRCLE that has a circumference of one mile (i.e., the two endpoints 0 and 1 are now connected to each other). Is the location configuration in question (b) a Nash equilibrium? Please explain your answer.
Yes, it is now a NE. [3 points]
Given the other two vendors’ locations, a vendor’s market share cannot exceed 1/3 no matter where it moves to. [2 points]
	•	[5 points] Return to the original case where the beach is a line segment. Suppose that vendor 1 has already entered the market and located at the left endpoint 0. Now vendors 2 and 3 simultaneously choose where to locate. What is the Nah equilibrium of the location choice game between vendors 2 and 3?
Both vendors 2 and 3 will locate at 2/3 in the NE. [5 points; no explanation needed.] It is easy to verify that none of them has an individual incentive to move away from this location.
(Here is a way to show this is the unique NE. It is clearly not a NE if vendors 2 and 3 are not in the same location. Suppose now they are at the same location, say, x. Obviously it is not a NE if x=0. Suppose then x>0. In that case, they will each have a market share 0.5(1-0.5x). If one of them, say, vendor 2 moves slightly to the right of vendor 3, it will have a market share almost equal to 1-x; if vendor 2 moves to the left of vendor 3, it will have a market share of 0.5x. In a NE, neither of the deviation should be profitable. You can easily check that this requires x=2/3.)

Inverse demand before subsidy:

               Equilibrium quantity: 0.12q=7, q*=58.33.                 Equilibrium price paid by consumers: 6-0.02*58.33=4.83.                Equilibrium price received by producers: 5.83.                 Total subsidy=1*58.33=58.33.                Deadweight loss: ½*1*(58.33-50)=4.165.

detected_equations: Revenues are P(Q) * Q = 12Q – 2/25Q^2, so marginal revenues equal 12 – 4/25 Q.
MR = MC when Q=62.5 and P = 7.
Profits are equal to P*Q – FC - VC * Q = 7*62.5-2*62.5 = 312.5.
For business, MR = MC implies Q = 22.5 and P = 11. The profit is equal to 202.5.
For students, MR = MC implies Q = 40 and P = 6. The profit is equal to 160.

given_solution:
Problem 2 (24 points) 

CalTrain has a local monopoly in the market for railway services between Palo Alto and San Francisco. The inverse demand for rides is given by P(Q) =12-(2/25)Q, where Q is the number of tickets sold and P is the price of a ride. CalTrain incurs in variable cost of $2 per train ride and has fixed costs of zero.
	•	(6 points) Calculate the profit maximizing price and quantity for Caltrain. 

Revenues are P(Q) * Q = 12Q – 2/25Q^2, so marginal revenues equal 12 – 4/25 Q.
Marginal costs equal 2.
MR = MC when Q=62.5 and P = 7.

	•	(6 points) What are the company’s profits? What is consumer surplus? What is the deadweight loss?

Profits are equal to P*Q – FC - VC * Q = 7*62.5-2*62.5 = 312.5.

The consumer surplus is the area between the demand curve and the monopoly price. The base equals monopoly demand, i.e., 62.5. The height is the difference between the intercept of the inverse demand curve (12) and the price (7), i.e., 5. The area is thus 5*62.5/2=156.25.

The deadweight loss can be found as the area of the triangle with height the difference between price and marginal cost (7-2=5) and base the difference in the monopoly quantity (62.5) and the quantity under perfect competition. To find the latter we can just equate demand to supply, i.e., marginal cost): 12-2/25Q=2, which implies Q = 125. Hence, the area of the deadweight loss triangle is (125-62.5)*5/2 = 156.25.
  
[Note: Give half credit to students who identify the areas graphically but do not calculate them.]


	•	(6 points) Suppose that, upon further market research, CalTrain finds out that they are serving two distinct groups of customers: Business, with an inverse demand of P(Q) = 20-0.4Q, and Students, with an inverse demand of P(Q) = 10-0.1Q. If CalTrain can price-discriminate, what would be the prices offered to the two groups of customers? What is the total number of tickets sold? What is the profit?

We can treat the two markets as two separate monopolistic markets.

For business, MR = MC implies Q = 22.5 and P = 11. The profit is equal to 202.5.

For students, MR = MC implies Q = 40 and P = 6. The profit is equal to 160.

With price discrimination, total profits equal 160+202.5 = 362.5. 

	•	(2 points) Given your answers to points a), b), and c), should CalTrain price-discriminate or not? Explain.

Yes, since profits are larger in d).

	•	(4 points) Suppose CalTrain is now able to engage in perfect price discrimination. Considering the total inverse demand of point (a), what would be the company’s profits and what would be consumer surplus?

Under perfect price discrimination, consumer surplus is zero and profits correspond to the area of the triangle below the demand curve and above marginal cost. Since total quantity sold would be where demand equals supply, the base of the triangle is 125. The height of the triangle is the intercept between the intercept of the inverse demand curve (12) and marginal costs (2), i.e., 10. Total profits would thus be 10*125/2 = 625. Note that this is also the sum of the deadweight loss, profit, and consumer surplus in point b).
problem_text:
Problem 2 (24 points) 

CalTrain has a local monopoly in the market for railway services between Palo Alto and San Francisco. The inverse demand for rides is given by P(Q) =12-(2/25)Q, where Q is the number of tickets sold and P is the price of a ride. CalTrain incurs in variable cost of $2 per train ride and has fixed costs of zero.
	•	(6 points) Calculate the profit maximizing price and quantity for Caltrain. 

Revenues are P(Q) * Q = 12Q – 2/25Q^2, so marginal revenues equal 12 – 4/25 Q.
Marginal costs equal 2.
MR = MC when Q=62.5 and P = 7.

	•	(6 points) What are the company’s profits? What is consumer surplus? What is the deadweight loss?

Profits are equal to P*Q – FC - VC * Q = 7*62.5-2*62.5 = 312.5.

The consumer surplus is the area between the demand curve and the monopoly price. The base equals monopoly demand, i.e., 62.5. The height is the difference between the intercept of the inverse demand curve (12) and the price (7), i.e., 5. The area is thus 5*62.5/2=156.25.

The deadweight loss can be found as the area of the triangle with height the difference between price and marginal cost (7-2=5) and base the difference in the monopoly quantity (62.5) and the quantity under perfect competition. To find the latter we can just equate demand to supply, i.e., marginal cost): 12-2/25Q=2, which implies Q = 125. Hence, the area of the deadweight loss triangle is (125-62.5)*5/2 = 156.25.
  
[Note: Give half credit to students who identify the areas graphically but do not calculate them.]


	•	(6 points) Suppose that, upon further market research, CalTrain finds out that they are serving two distinct groups of customers: Business, with an inverse demand of P(Q) = 20-0.4Q, and Students, with an inverse demand of P(Q) = 10-0.1Q. If CalTrain can price-discriminate, what would be the prices offered to the two groups of customers? What is the total number of tickets sold? What is the profit?

We can treat the two markets as two separate monopolistic markets.

For business, MR = MC implies Q = 22.5 and P = 11. The profit is equal to 202.5.

For students, MR = MC implies Q = 40 and P = 6. The profit is equal to 160.

With price discrimination, total profits equal 160+202.5 = 362.5. 

	•	(2 points) Given your answers to points a), b), and c), should CalTrain price-discriminate or not? Explain.

Yes, since profits are larger in d).

	•	(4 points) Suppose CalTrain is now able to engage in perfect price discrimination. Considering the total inverse demand of point (a), what would be the company’s profits and what would be consumer surplus?

Under perfect price discrimination, consumer surplus is zero and profits correspond to the area of the triangle below the demand curve and above marginal cost. Since total quantity sold would be where demand equals supply, the base of the triangle is 125. The height of the triangle is the intercept between the intercept of the inverse demand curve (12) and marginal costs (2), i.e., 10. Total profits would thus be 10*125/2 = 625. Note that this is also the sum of the deadweight loss, profit, and consumer surplus in point b).

given_solution:
Problem 3 (21 points) 

A friend is deciding between two job offers: one from firm Flat, which pays a flat wage of $95,000, and one from firm P4P, which pays a base salary of $75,000 and a bonus of $45,000 upon reaching a performance target. Suppose your friend assigns a 50% probability to reaching the performance target.

	•	(2 points) What is the expected value of the two jobs to your friend? 

Flat: $95,000
P4P: $75,000 + 0.5 * 45,000 = $97,500

	•	(2 points) If your friend is risk-neutral, what job would they pick?

They would pick the P4P job (utility = expected value, so they pick the job with the highest expected value).

	•	(3 points) Would your answer change if your friend is risk-averse, with a utility function u(x)=  ?

Now utility is not equal to expected value anymore. 
Expected utility of Flat: 308.2207
Expected utility of P2P: 0.5310.1357

The answer would not change; EU of P4P > EU of Flat.
Suppose now that you are a HR manager at firm P4P and are deciding whether to change your compensation scheme. Namely, you are considering adjusting your base salary and bonus to incentivize employees to work hard. Suppose your employees can choose two levels of effort: “low”, which gives them a chance equal to 30% of meeting the performance target, and “high”, which gives them a chance equal to 70%. The cost of effort is equal to $30,000. Assume, as above, that your main competitor – firm Flat – is paying employees a flat wage of $95,000.

	•	(6 points) Assuming all employees are risk-neutral, find the optimal base salary and bonus you would have to pay if you wanted to implement the bonus scheme.

The setup is identical to what we have seen in class, with S=0.7, n=0.3, c=30,000, and m = 95,000.

The optimal bonus is b* = c/(S-n) = 30,000/0.4 = $75,000

The optimal base salary is w = m – n*b = 95,000 – 0.3*75,000 = $72,500


	•	(2 points) Would your answers to point d) change if you found out your employees where risk averse? Why?

The answer to point d) assumes that workers are risk averse. If workers are risk-neutral, they would have to be compensated for the risk involved in the bonus scheme, so at least one between w and b would have to be higher.
	•	(6 points) Suppose that, upon further research, you find out that the profit margins from meeting the performance target is $60,000 per worker (that is, each worker meeting the target increases your profits, net of all non-labor costs, by $60,000). Would it be worth it for your company to stick with the bonus scheme, or would it be better off to switch to the flat wage scheme? (For this point, assume again workers are risk-neutral). Explain.

We saw in class that the per-worker, per-unit of output (in this case, the performance target) profit margin would have to be at least as great as the bonus in order to justify the bonus scheme being used. Given that we found a bonus of $75,000 in point d), a margin of $60,000 is too low and P4P is better off offering the same flat wage as the competitor.

Problem 4: Price Competition and Capacity [20 points]
Consider a one-shot price competition between Apple and Samsung for their new smartphones. Each company can chose to charge either $800 or $1,000. The following is the DEMAND matrix (not the profit matrix):
                                        Samsung
 Apple

$800
$1000

$800
30, 20
35, 10

$1000
18, 22
21, 18

For example, if both firms set a price of $800, Apple will have a demand of 30 million units and Samsung will have a demand of 20 million units. (While the demand numbers are in Millions, you don’t need to account for that in your calculations.) Suppose the production cost per unit is $400, the same for both companies. (We don’t consider fixed costs as they usually don’t impact price competition.) Assume each company aims to maximize its own profits.
a)  Suppose first that the two companies set their prices simultaneously. Please answer the following questions. [Hint: Start by constructing the game matrix with profit numbers. Don’t forget to include costs, and be sure to double check your calculations before proceeding.]

(a-1) Does Samsung have a dominant strategy? If so, please specify. [4 points]
(a-2) What prices will the two companies set in the Nash Equilibrium? [4 points]

                                        Samsung
 Apple

$800
$1000

$800
12k, 8k
14k, 6k

$1000
10.8k, 8.8k
12.6k, 10.8k

All the profit numbers are calculated as profit=demand×(price-cost).
(a-1) Samsung has no dominant strategy. [4 points]
(a-2) In NE, each firm charges $800. (This is easy to see by noticing that Apple has a dominant strategy $800, or by the best response method.) [2 points for each firm’s price]
b) Suppose now that Apple announces its price first, and then Samsung responds. Please answer the following questions. [Hint: Start by drawing the game tree.]
(b-1) What prices will the two companies set? [4 points]
(b-2) If instead Samsung announces its price first, how will each company’s profit change? [4 points]




(b-1) Both firms will charge $1000. [2 points for each firm’s price]
(b-2) In that case, both firms will charge $800. Apple’s profit will drop from 12.6k to 12k, and Samsung’s profit will drop from 10.8k to 8k.  [2 points for each firm; it is ok if they just say each firm’s profit drops.]

c) Return to the simultaneous-move game, but now suppose Apple has a capacity constraint of 25 million units, while Samsung does not. In other words, Apple cannot serve more than 25 million customers, and any unserved customers will buy from Samsung. Please answer the following questions. [Hint: You need to re-draw a game matrix.]
(c-1) What prices will the two companies now set in the Nash Equilibrium? [2 points]
(c-2) Compared to the situation in question (a), does Apple suffer or benefit from its capacity constraint, and by how much? [2 points]
                                        Samsung
 Apple

$800
$1000

$800
10k, 10k
10k, 12k

$1000
10.8k, 8.8k
12.6k, 10.8k

(c-1) Both charge $1000. [1 point for each firm’s price]
(c-2) Apple benefits from its capacity constraint by 0.6k. [2 points] (This is because the capacity constraint softens price competition.)

detected_equations: • If X=5 and Y=8, does any player have a dominant or dominated strategy? [3 points]

given_solution:
Problem 5: A game with some unknown payoffs [15 points]

Consider the following simultaneous-move game with two unknown payoff numbers X and Y. X≠3, Y≠7, and they are integers.

                                    Player 2
Player 1

L
C
R

T
9, 5
X, 6
2, 4

M
6, 5
2, 4
1 ,3

B
2, 7
3, 6
4, Y

	•	If X=5 and Y=8, does any player have a dominant or dominated strategy? [3 points]

Player 1 has a dominated strategy M.

	•	For what values of X and Y does the game have two Nash equilibria (NE)? [6 points] [Hint: find all possible values of X and Y, and express your answers in inequalities.]

 X≥3 and Y≥7 (or X>3 and Y>7). [3 points for each value.] 

	•	For what values of X and Y does the game have a unique NE that predicts the same outcome as in the sequential-move version of this game, where player 1 moves first? [6 points]
[Hint: find all possible values of X and Y; try to use backward induction without drawing the game tree for the sequential game.]

X>6 and Y<7. [3 points for each value] (Give full points if they use weak inequalities.)

There are two possible cases where the simultaneous game has a unique NE: (X≥3, Y<7) in which case the NE is (T, C), and (X<3, Y≥7) in which case the NE is (B, R).

In the sequential-move game:
if player 1 chooses T, then player 2 will choose C, so player 1 will get X;
if player 1 chooses M, then player 2 will choose L, so player 1 will get 6;
if player 1 chooses B, then player 2 will choose L or R, depending on whether Y<7 or Y>7, so player 1 will get 2 or 4. In either case the outcome is worse for player 1 than 6 when she chooses M.
Therefore, player 1 will NEVER choose B. The outcome is either (T, C) if X>6 or (M, L) if X<6.

Therefore, to make the simultaneous and the sequential game have the same (unique) prediction, the only possibility is that (T, C) happens in either case. 

For (T, C) to be the unique NE in the simultaneous game, as pointed out before, we need (X≥3, Y<7); for the same outcome to happen in the sequential game, we need X>6. Therefore, the condition we are seeking is (X>6, Y<7).

problem_title: A game with some unknown payoffs [15 points]

given_solution:
Problem 1 (20 points)

From the second quarter of 2020 to the fourth quarter of 2022, the median home sales price went up by 48%. This question explores a possible explanation for the rising cost of single-family houses. 

Suppose that, in 2020, the demand for houses was:

Meanwhile, the supply of houses in 2020 was: 


	•	(3 points) What is the equilibrium price and quantity of the housing market in 2020?

Price=1; quantity=50  
In 2020, people started to spend a lot more time at home because of Covid. Therefore, many people wanted to scale up their houses. Moreover, the booming stock market gave a lot of cash to individual investors, causing them to have higher willingness to pay. This leads to an increase in demand. Suppose that the demand for houses in 2022 became: 


	•	(3 points) What would the equilibrium price and quantity be if the supply had stayed the same?  Price=3; quantity=150 
 Moreover, the supply chain problem made it difficult and more costly for new constructions to gather materials. Social distancing and other Covid prevention measures also made any in-person work in the construction process (e.g., wiring, plumbing, and application of permits etc) difficult. These caused the supply to decrease. Suppose that the supply in 2022 became:  

  
	•	(4 points) What is the equilibrium price and quantity in 2022?  Price=5; quantity=50
 To make housing more affordable, suppose that the government decides to provide subsidies to home buyers. 
If there is a $1 per-unit subsidy to home buyers, what would the equilibrium price and quantity be? What is the total expenditure? What is the deadweight loss?  
	•	(5 points) If there is a $1 per-unit subsidy to home buyers, what would the equilibrium price and quantity be? What is the total expenditure? What is the deadweight loss?
Inverse demand before subsidy: 

               Inverse demand after subsidy: 
               Inverse supply: 


               Equilibrium quantity: 0.12q=7, q*=58.33.                 Equilibrium price paid by consumers: 6-0.02*58.33=4.83.                Equilibrium price received by producers: 5.83.                 Total subsidy=1*58.33=58.33.                Deadweight loss: ½*1*(58.33-50)=4.165.   
	•	(5 points) Would it be better for the producers if they were subsidized $1 per unit instead of the consumers? Why or why not? Which side of the market benefits more from subsidies?   Subsidizing the producers would be the same as subsidizing the consumers. Demand elasticity in the 2022 equilibrium is 50*5/50=5. Supply elasticity in the 2022 equilibrium is 10*5/50=1. Demand is more elastic, so consumers always benefit more regardless which side is subsidized.

Problem 1 (20 points)

From the second quarter of 2020 to the fourth quarter of 2022, the median home sales price went
up by 48%. This question explores a possible explanation for the rising cost of single-family houses.

Suppose that, in 2020, the demand for houses was:
                                       𝑄! (𝑝) = 150 − 100𝑝
Meanwhile, the supply of houses in 2020 was:
                                           𝑄"⬚ (𝑝) = 50𝑝.

   a) (3 points) What is the equilibrium price and quantity of the housing market in 2020?

       Price=1; quantity=50


In 2020, people started to spend a lot more time at home because of Covid. Therefore, many people
wanted to scale up their houses. Moreover, the booming stock market gave a lot of cash to
individual investors, causing them to have higher willingness to pay. This leads to an increase in
demand. Suppose that the demand for houses in 2022 became:
                                        𝑄! (𝑝) = 300 − 50𝑝

   b) (3 points) What would the equilibrium price and quantity be if the supply had stayed the
      same?
      Price=3; quantity=150


Moreover, the supply chain problem made it difficult and more costly for new constructions to
gather materials. Social distancing and other Covid prevention measures also made any in-person
work in the construction process (e.g., wiring, plumbing, and application of permits etc) difficult.
These caused the supply to decrease. Suppose that the supply in 2022 became:
                                            𝑄"⬚ (𝑝) = 10𝑝.



   c) (4 points) What is the equilibrium price and quantity in 2022?
      Price=5; quantity=50

To make housing more affordable, suppose that the government decides to provide subsidies to
home buyers.

                                                  4


  MGT 404 2023                                                                                      Final Exam



If there is a $1 per-unit subsidy to home buyers, what would the equilibrium price and quantity be?
What is the total expenditure? What is the deadweight loss?

    d) (5 points) If there is a $1 per-unit subsidy to home buyers, what would the equilibrium
       price and quantity be? What is the total expenditure? What is the deadweight loss?
       Inverse demand before subsidy:
                                          𝑃! (𝑞) = 6 − 0.02𝑞
       Inverse demand after subsidy:
                                          𝑃! (𝑞) = 7 − 0.02𝑞
       Inverse supply:
                                             𝑃"⬚ (𝑞) = 0.1𝑞

        Equilibrium quantity: 0.12q=7, q*=58.33.
        Equilibrium price paid by consumers: 6-0.02*58.33=4.83.
        Equilibrium price received by producers: 5.83.
        Total subsidy=1*58.33=58.33.
        Deadweight loss: ½*1*(58.33-50)=4.165.


    e) (5 points) Would it be better for the producers if they were subsidized $1 per unit instead of
       the consumers? Why or why not? Which side of the market benefits more from subsidies?

        Subsidizing the producers would be the same as subsidizing the consumers. Demand
        elasticity in the 2022 equilibrium is 50*5/50=5. Supply elasticity in the 2022 equilibrium is
        10*5/50=1. Demand is more elastic, so consumers always benefit more regardless which
        side is subsidized.

demand. Suppose that the demand for houses in 2022 became:
                                        𝑄! (𝑝) = 300 − 50𝑝

Moreover, the supply chain problem made it difficult and more costly for new constructions to

To make housing more affordable, suppose that the government decides to provide subsidies to

       Inverse demand before subsidy:
                                          𝑃! (𝑞) = 6 − 0.02𝑞

                                          𝑃! (𝑞) = 7 − 0.02𝑞

        Equilibrium price paid by consumers: 6-0.02*58.33=4.83.

subparts.label: e
subparts.text:
e) (5 points) Would it be better for the producers if they were subsidized $1 per unit instead of
       the consumers? Why or why not? Which side of the market benefits more from subsidies?

        Subsidizing the producers would be the same as subsidizing the consumers. Demand
        elasticity in the 2022 equilibrium is 50*5/50=5. Supply elasticity in the 2022 equilibrium is
        10*5/50=1. Demand is more elastic, so consumers always benefit more regardless which
        side is subsidized.

given_solution:
Problem 2 (24 points) 

CalTrain has a local monopoly in the market for railway services between Palo Alto and San Francisco. The inverse demand for rides is given by P(Q) =12-(2/25)Q, where Q is the number of tickets sold and P is the price of a ride. CalTrain incurs in variable cost of $2 per train ride and has fixed costs of zero.
	•	(6 points) Calculate the profit maximizing price and quantity for Caltrain. 

Revenues are P(Q) * Q = 12Q – 2/25Q^2, so marginal revenues equal 12 – 4/25 Q.
Marginal costs equal 2.
MR = MC when Q=62.5 and P = 7.

	•	(6 points) What are the company’s profits? What is consumer surplus? What is the deadweight loss?

Profits are equal to P*Q – FC - VC * Q = 7*62.5-2*62.5 = 312.5.

The consumer surplus is the area between the demand curve and the monopoly price. The base equals monopoly demand, i.e., 62.5. The height is the difference between the intercept of the inverse demand curve (12) and the price (7), i.e., 5. The area is thus 5*62.5/2=156.25.

The deadweight loss can be found as the area of the triangle with height the difference between price and marginal cost (7-2=5) and base the difference in the monopoly quantity (62.5) and the quantity under perfect competition. To find the latter we can just equate demand to supply, i.e., marginal cost): 12-2/25Q=2, which implies Q = 125. Hence, the area of the deadweight loss triangle is (125-62.5)*5/2 = 156.25.
  
[Note: Give half credit to students who identify the areas graphically but do not calculate them.]


	•	(6 points) Suppose that, upon further market research, CalTrain finds out that they are serving two distinct groups of customers: Business, with an inverse demand of P(Q) = 20-0.4Q, and Students, with an inverse demand of P(Q) = 10-0.1Q. If CalTrain can price-discriminate, what would be the prices offered to the two groups of customers? What is the total number of tickets sold? What is the profit?

We can treat the two markets as two separate monopolistic markets.

For business, MR = MC implies Q = 22.5 and P = 11. The profit is equal to 202.5.

For students, MR = MC implies Q = 40 and P = 6. The profit is equal to 160.

With price discrimination, total profits equal 160+202.5 = 362.5. 

	•	(2 points) Given your answers to points a), b), and c), should CalTrain price-discriminate or not? Explain.

Yes, since profits are larger in d).

	•	(4 points) Suppose CalTrain is now able to engage in perfect price discrimination. Considering the total inverse demand of point (a), what would be the company’s profits and what would be consumer surplus?

Under perfect price discrimination, consumer surplus is zero and profits correspond to the area of the triangle below the demand curve and above marginal cost. Since total quantity sold would be where demand equals supply, the base of the triangle is 125. The height of the triangle is the intercept between the intercept of the inverse demand curve (12) and marginal costs (2), i.e., 10. Total profits would thus be 10*125/2 = 625. Note that this is also the sum of the deadweight loss, profit, and consumer surplus in point b).

detected_equations: They would pick the P4P job (utility = expected value, so they pick the job with the highest
The setup is identical to what we have seen in class, with S=0.7, n=0.3, c=30,000, and m = 95,000.
The optimal bonus is b* = c/(S-n) = 30,000/0.4 = $75,000
The optimal base salary is w = m – n*b = 95,000 – 0.3*75,000 = $72,500

given_solution:
Problem 3 (21 points) 

A friend is deciding between two job offers: one from firm Flat, which pays a flat wage of $95,000, and one from firm P4P, which pays a base salary of $75,000 and a bonus of $45,000 upon reaching a performance target. Suppose your friend assigns a 50% probability to reaching the performance target.

	•	(2 points) What is the expected value of the two jobs to your friend? 

Flat: $95,000
P4P: $75,000 + 0.5 * 45,000 = $97,500

	•	(2 points) If your friend is risk-neutral, what job would they pick?

They would pick the P4P job (utility = expected value, so they pick the job with the highest expected value).

	•	(3 points) Would your answer change if your friend is risk-averse, with a utility function u(x)=  ?

Now utility is not equal to expected value anymore. 
Expected utility of Flat: 308.2207
Expected utility of P2P: 0.5310.1357

The answer would not change; EU of P4P > EU of Flat.
Suppose now that you are a HR manager at firm P4P and are deciding whether to change your compensation scheme. Namely, you are considering adjusting your base salary and bonus to incentivize employees to work hard. Suppose your employees can choose two levels of effort: “low”, which gives them a chance equal to 30% of meeting the performance target, and “high”, which gives them a chance equal to 70%. The cost of effort is equal to $30,000. Assume, as above, that your main competitor – firm Flat – is paying employees a flat wage of $95,000.

	•	(6 points) Assuming all employees are risk-neutral, find the optimal base salary and bonus you would have to pay if you wanted to implement the bonus scheme.

The setup is identical to what we have seen in class, with S=0.7, n=0.3, c=30,000, and m = 95,000.

The optimal bonus is b* = c/(S-n) = 30,000/0.4 = $75,000

The optimal base salary is w = m – n*b = 95,000 – 0.3*75,000 = $72,500


	•	(2 points) Would your answers to point d) change if you found out your employees where risk averse? Why?

The answer to point d) assumes that workers are risk averse. If workers are risk-neutral, they would have to be compensated for the risk involved in the bonus scheme, so at least one between w and b would have to be higher.
	•	(6 points) Suppose that, upon further research, you find out that the profit margins from meeting the performance target is $60,000 per worker (that is, each worker meeting the target increases your profits, net of all non-labor costs, by $60,000). Would it be worth it for your company to stick with the bonus scheme, or would it be better off to switch to the flat wage scheme? (For this point, assume again workers are risk-neutral). Explain.

We saw in class that the per-worker, per-unit of output (in this case, the performance target) profit margin would have to be at least as great as the bonus in order to justify the bonus scheme being used. Given that we found a bonus of $75,000 in point d), a margin of $60,000 is too low and P4P is better off offering the same flat wage as the competitor.

problem_text:
Problem 3 (21 points)

A friend is deciding between two job offers: one from firm Flat, which pays a flat wage of $95,000,
and one from firm P4P, which pays a base salary of $75,000 and a bonus of $45,000 upon reaching a
performance target. Suppose your friend assigns a 50% probability to reaching the performance
target.

   a) (2 points) What is the expected value of the two jobs to your friend?

       Flat: $95,000
       P4P: $75,000 + 0.5 * 45,000 = $97,500

   b) (2 points) If your friend is risk-neutral, what job would they pick?

       They would pick the P4P job (utility = expected value, so they pick the job with the highest
       expected value).

   c) (3 points) Would your answer change if your friend is risk-averse, with a utility function
      u(x)= √𝑥 ?

       Now utility is not equal to expected value anymore.

       Expected utility of Flat: 795,000 =308.2207
       Expected utility of P2P: 0.5775,000 + 0.5 ∗ √120,000 =310.1357

       The answer would not change; EU of P4P > EU of Flat.

Suppose now that you are a HR manager at firm P4P and are deciding whether to change your
compensation scheme. Namely, you are considering adjusting your base salary and bonus to
incentivize employees to work hard. Suppose your employees can choose two levels of effort: “low”,
which gives them a chance equal to 30% of meeting the performance target, and “high”, which gives
them a chance equal to 70%. The cost of effort is equal to $30,000. Assume, as above, that your
main competitor – firm Flat – is paying employees a flat wage of $95,000.

   d) (6 points) Assuming all employees are risk-neutral, find the optimal base salary and bonus
      you would have to pay if you wanted to implement the bonus scheme.

The setup is identical to what we have seen in class, with S=0.7, n=0.3, c=30,000, and m = 95,000.

                                                 7


  MGT 404 2023                                                                                  Final Exam




The optimal bonus is b* = c/(S-n) = 30,000/0.4 = $75,000

The optimal base salary is w = m – n*b = 95,000 – 0.3*75,000 = $72,500



   a) (2 points) Would your answers to point d) change if you found out your employees where
      risk averse? Why?

       The answer to point d) assumes that workers are risk averse. If workers are risk-neutral,
       they would have to be compensated for the risk involved in the bonus scheme, so at least
       one between w and b would have to be higher.

   b) (6 points) Suppose that, upon further research, you find out that the profit margins from
      meeting the performance target is $60,000 per worker (that is, each worker meeting the
      target increases your profits, net of all non-labor costs, by $60,000). Would it be worth it for
      your company to stick with the bonus scheme, or would it be better off to switch to the flat
      wage scheme? (For this point, assume again workers are risk-neutral). Explain.

We saw in class that the per-worker, per-unit of output (in this case, the performance target) profit
margin would have to be at least as great as the bonus in order to justify the bonus scheme being
used. Given that we found a bonus of $75,000 in point d), a margin of $60,000 is too low and P4P is
better off offering the same flat wage as the competitor.

detected_equations: All the profit numbers are calculated as profit=demand×(price-cost).

given_solution:
Problem 4: Price Competition and Capacity [20 points]
Consider a one-shot price competition between Apple and Samsung for their new smartphones. Each company can chose to charge either $800 or $1,000. The following is the DEMAND matrix (not the profit matrix):
                                        Samsung
 Apple

$800
$1000

$800
30, 20
35, 10

$1000
18, 22
21, 18

For example, if both firms set a price of $800, Apple will have a demand of 30 million units and Samsung will have a demand of 20 million units. (While the demand numbers are in Millions, you don’t need to account for that in your calculations.) Suppose the production cost per unit is $400, the same for both companies. (We don’t consider fixed costs as they usually don’t impact price competition.) Assume each company aims to maximize its own profits.
a)  Suppose first that the two companies set their prices simultaneously. Please answer the following questions. [Hint: Start by constructing the game matrix with profit numbers. Don’t forget to include costs, and be sure to double check your calculations before proceeding.]

(a-1) Does Samsung have a dominant strategy? If so, please specify. [4 points]
(a-2) What prices will the two companies set in the Nash Equilibrium? [4 points]

                                        Samsung
 Apple

$800
$1000

$800
12k, 8k
14k, 6k

$1000
10.8k, 8.8k
12.6k, 10.8k

All the profit numbers are calculated as profit=demand×(price-cost).
(a-1) Samsung has no dominant strategy. [4 points]
(a-2) In NE, each firm charges $800. (This is easy to see by noticing that Apple has a dominant strategy $800, or by the best response method.) [2 points for each firm’s price]
b) Suppose now that Apple announces its price first, and then Samsung responds. Please answer the following questions. [Hint: Start by drawing the game tree.]
(b-1) What prices will the two companies set? [4 points]
(b-2) If instead Samsung announces its price first, how will each company’s profit change? [4 points]




(b-1) Both firms will charge $1000. [2 points for each firm’s price]
(b-2) In that case, both firms will charge $800. Apple’s profit will drop from 12.6k to 12k, and Samsung’s profit will drop from 10.8k to 8k.  [2 points for each firm; it is ok if they just say each firm’s profit drops.]

c) Return to the simultaneous-move game, but now suppose Apple has a capacity constraint of 25 million units, while Samsung does not. In other words, Apple cannot serve more than 25 million customers, and any unserved customers will buy from Samsung. Please answer the following questions. [Hint: You need to re-draw a game matrix.]
(c-1) What prices will the two companies now set in the Nash Equilibrium? [2 points]
(c-2) Compared to the situation in question (a), does Apple suffer or benefit from its capacity constraint, and by how much? [2 points]
                                        Samsung
 Apple

$800
$1000

$800
10k, 10k
10k, 12k

$1000
10.8k, 8.8k
12.6k, 10.8k

(c-1) Both charge $1000. [1 point for each firm’s price]
(c-2) Apple benefits from its capacity constraint by 0.6k. [2 points] (This is because the capacity constraint softens price competition.)

given_solution:
Problem 5: A game with some unknown payoffs [15 points]

Consider the following simultaneous-move game with two unknown payoff numbers X and Y. X≠3, Y≠7, and they are integers.

                                    Player 2
Player 1

L
C
R

T
9, 5
X, 6
2, 4

M
6, 5
2, 4
1 ,3

B
2, 7
3, 6
4, Y

	•	If X=5 and Y=8, does any player have a dominant or dominated strategy? [3 points]

Player 1 has a dominated strategy M.

	•	For what values of X and Y does the game have two Nash equilibria (NE)? [6 points] [Hint: find all possible values of X and Y, and express your answers in inequalities.]

 X≥3 and Y≥7 (or X>3 and Y>7). [3 points for each value.] 

	•	For what values of X and Y does the game have a unique NE that predicts the same outcome as in the sequential-move version of this game, where player 1 moves first? [6 points]
[Hint: find all possible values of X and Y; try to use backward induction without drawing the game tree for the sequential game.]

X>6 and Y<7. [3 points for each value] (Give full points if they use weak inequalities.)

There are two possible cases where the simultaneous game has a unique NE: (X≥3, Y<7) in which case the NE is (T, C), and (X<3, Y≥7) in which case the NE is (B, R).

In the sequential-move game:
if player 1 chooses T, then player 2 will choose C, so player 1 will get X;
if player 1 chooses M, then player 2 will choose L, so player 1 will get 6;
if player 1 chooses B, then player 2 will choose L or R, depending on whether Y<7 or Y>7, so player 1 will get 2 or 4. In either case the outcome is worse for player 1 than 6 when she chooses M.
Therefore, player 1 will NEVER choose B. The outcome is either (T, C) if X>6 or (M, L) if X<6.

Therefore, to make the simultaneous and the sequential game have the same (unique) prediction, the only possibility is that (T, C) happens in either case. 

For (T, C) to be the unique NE in the simultaneous game, as pointed out before, we need (X≥3, Y<7); for the same outcome to happen in the sequential game, we need X>6. Therefore, the condition we are seeking is (X>6, Y<7).

In the sequential-move game:

Problem 1 (20 points)

From the second quarter of 2020 to the fourth quarter of 2022, the median home sales price went up by 48%. This question explores a possible explanation for the rising cost of single-family houses. 

Suppose that, in 2020, the demand for houses was:

Meanwhile, the supply of houses in 2020 was: 


	•	(2 points) What is the equilibrium price and quantity of the housing market in 2020?

Price=2; quantity=100  
In 2020, people started to spend a lot more time at home because of Covid. Therefore, many people wanted to scale up their houses. Moreover, the booming stock market gave a lot of cash to individual investors, causing them to have higher willingness to pay. This leads to an increase in demand. Suppose that the demand for houses in 2022 became: 


	•	(2 points) What would the equilibrium price and quantity be if the supply had stayed the same?  Price=6; quantity=300 
 Moreover, the supply chain problem made it difficult and more costly for new constructions to gather materials. Social distancing and other Covid prevention measures also made any in-person work in the construction process (e.g., wiring, plumbing, and application of permits etc) difficult. These caused the supply to decrease. Suppose that the supply in 2022 became:  

  
	•	(4 points) What is the equilibrium price and quantity in 2022?  Price=10; quantity=100
 To make housing more affordable, suppose that the government decides to provide subsidies to home buyers. 
If there is a $1 per-unit subsidy to home buyers, what would the equilibrium price and quantity be? What is the total expenditure? What is the deadweight loss?  
	•	(5 points) If there is a $2 per-unit subsidy to home buyers, what would the equilibrium price and quantity be? What is the total expenditure? What is the deadweight loss?
Inverse demand before subsidy: 

               Inverse demand after subsidy: 
               Inverse supply: 


               Equilibrium quantity: 0.12q=14, q*=116.67.                 Equilibrium price paid by consumers: 12-0.02*116.67=9.67.                Equilibrium price received by producers: 11.67.                 Total subsidy=2*116.67=233.34.                Deadweight loss: ½*2*(116.67-100)=16.67.   
	•	(5 points) Would it be better for the producers if they were subsidized $2 per unit instead of the consumers? Why or why not? Which side of the market benefits more from subsidies?   Subsidizing the producers would be the same as subsidizing the consumers. Demand elasticity in the 2022 equilibrium is 50*10/100=5. Supply elasticity in the 2022 equilibrium is 10*10/100=1. Demand is more elastic, so consumers always benefit more regardless which side is subsidized.

given_solution:
Problem 2 (24 points) 

CalTrain has a local monopoly in the market for railway services between Palo Alto and San Francisco. The inverse demand for rides is given by P(Q) =(40/3)-(1/15)Q, where Q is the number of tickets sold and P is the price of a ride. CalTrain incurs in variable cost of $0 per train ride and has fixed costs of zero.
	•	(6 points) Calculate the profit maximizing price and quantity for Caltrain. 



MR = MC when Q=100 and P = 20/3=6.66667..

	•	(6 points) What are the company’s profits? What is consumer surplus? What is the deadweight loss?

Profits are equal to P*Q – FC - VC * Q = 100*20/3 = 666.66667.

The consumer surplus is the area between the demand curve and the monopoly price. The base equals monopoly demand, i.e., 100. The height is the difference between the intercept of the inverse demand curve (40/3) and the price (20/3), i.e., 20/3. The area is thus 0.5*100*20/3=333.333.

The deadweight loss can be found as the area of the triangle with height the difference between price and marginal cost (20/3-0) and base the difference in the monopoly quantity (100) and the quantity under perfect competition. To find the latter we can just equate demand to supply, i.e., marginal cost): 40/3-1/15Q=0, which implies Q = 200. Hence, the area of the deadweight loss triangle is (200-100)*20/3 = 666.67.
  
[Note: Give half credit to students who identify the areas graphically but do not calculate them.]


	•	(6 points) Suppose that, upon further market research, CalTrain finds out that they are serving two distinct groups of customers: Business, with an inverse demand of P(Q) = 15-(1/15)Q, and Students, with an inverse demand of P(Q) = 10-0.1Q. If CalTrain can price-discriminate, what would be the prices offered to the two groups of customers? What is the total number of tickets sold? What is the profit?

We can treat the two markets as two separate monopolistic markets.

For business, MR = MC implies Q = 112.5 and P = 7. The profit is equal to 787.5.

For students, MR = MC implies Q = 50 and P = 5. The profit is equal to 250.

With price discrimination, total profits equal 1037.5.

	•	(2 points) Given your answers to points a), b), and c), should CalTrain price-discriminate or not? Explain.

Yes, since profits are larger in d).

	•	(4 points) Suppose CalTrain is now able to engage in perfect price discrimination. Considering the total inverse demand of point (a), what would be the company’s profits and what would be consumer surplus?

Under perfect price discrimination, consumer surplus is zero and profits correspond to the area of the triangle below the demand curve and above marginal cost. Since total quantity sold would be where demand equals supply, the base of the triangle is 200. The height of the triangle is the intercept between the intercept of the inverse demand curve (40/3) and marginal costs (0), i.e., 40/3. Total profits would thus be 1333.33. Note that this is also the sum of the deadweight loss, profit, and consumer surplus in point b).

given_solution:
Problem 3 (21 points) 

A friend is deciding between two job offers: one from firm Flat, which pays a flat wage of $105,000, and one from firm P4P, which pays a base salary of $85,000 and a bonus of $35,000 upon reaching a performance target. Suppose your friend assigns a 40% probability to reaching the performance target.

	•	(2 points) What is the expected value of the two jobs to your friend? 

Flat: $105,000
P4P: $85,000 + 0.4 * 35,000 = $99,000

	•	(2 points) If your friend is risk-neutral, what job would they pick?

They would pick the P4P job (utility = expected value, so they pick the job with the highest expected value).

	•	(3 points) Would your answer change if your friend is risk-averse, with a utility function u(x)=  ?

Now utility is not equal to expected value anymore. 
Expected utility of Flat: 324.04
Expected utility of P2P: 0.6313.49

They would pick the Flat job.
Suppose now that you are a HR manager at firm P4P and are deciding whether to change your compensation scheme. Namely, you are considering adjusting your base salary and bonus to incentivize employees to work hard. Suppose your employees can choose two levels of effort: “low”, which gives them a chance equal to 30% of meeting the performance target, and “high”, which gives them a chance equal to 70%. The cost of effort is equal to $30,000. Assume, as above, that your main competitor – firm Flat – is paying employees a flat wage of $105,000.

	•	(6 points) Assuming all employees are risk-neutral, find the optimal base salary and bonus you would have to pay if you wanted to implement the bonus scheme.

The setup is identical to what we have seen in class, with S=0.7, n=0.3, c=30,000, and m = 105,000.

The optimal bonus is b* = c/(S-n) = 30,000/0.4 = $75,000

The optimal base salary is w = m – n*b = 105,000 – 0.3*75,000 = $82,500


	•	(2 points) Would your answers to point d) change if you found out your employees where risk averse? Why?

The answer to point d) assumes that workers are risk averse. If workers are risk-neutral, they would have to be compensated for the risk involved in the bonus scheme, so at least one between w and b would have to be higher.
	•	(6 points) Suppose that, upon further research, you find out that the profit margins from meeting the performance target is $60,000 per worker (that is, each worker meeting the target increases your profits, net of all non-labor costs, by $80,000). Would it be worth it for your company to stick with the bonus scheme, or would it be better off to switch to the flat wage scheme? (For this point, assume again workers are risk-neutral). Explain.

We saw in class that the per-worker, per-unit of output (in this case, the performance target) profit margin would have to be at least as great as the bonus in order to justify the bonus scheme being used. Given that we found a bonus of $75,000 in point d), a margin of $80,000 is high enough and P4P is better off offering the the bonus scheme.

Problem 1 (20 points)

From the second quarter of 2020 to the fourth quarter of 2022, the median home sales price went up by 48%. This question explores a possible explanation for the rising cost of single-family houses. 

Suppose that, in 2020, the demand for houses was:

Meanwhile, the supply of houses in 2020 was: 


	•	(2 points) What is the equilibrium price and quantity of the housing market in 2020?

Price=2; quantity=100  
In 2020, people started to spend a lot more time at home because of Covid. Therefore, many people wanted to scale up their houses. Moreover, the booming stock market gave a lot of cash to individual investors, causing them to have higher willingness to pay. This leads to an increase in demand. Suppose that the demand for houses in 2022 became: 


	•	(2 points) What would the equilibrium price and quantity be if the supply had stayed the same?  Price=6; quantity=300 
 Moreover, the supply chain problem made it difficult and more costly for new constructions to gather materials. Social distancing and other Covid prevention measures also made any in-person work in the construction process (e.g., wiring, plumbing, and application of permits etc) difficult. These caused the supply to decrease. Suppose that the supply in 2022 became:  

  
	•	(4 points) What is the equilibrium price and quantity in 2022?  Price=10; quantity=100
 To make housing more affordable, suppose that the government decides to provide subsidies to home buyers. 
If there is a $1 per-unit subsidy to home buyers, what would the equilibrium price and quantity be? What is the total expenditure? What is the deadweight loss?  
	•	(5 points) If there is a $2 per-unit subsidy to home buyers, what would the equilibrium price and quantity be? What is the total expenditure? What is the deadweight loss?
Inverse demand before subsidy: 

               Inverse demand after subsidy: 
               Inverse supply: 


               Equilibrium quantity: 0.12q=14, q*=116.67.                 Equilibrium price paid by consumers: 12-0.02*116.67=9.67.                Equilibrium price received by producers: 11.67.                 Total subsidy=2*116.67=233.34.                Deadweight loss: ½*2*(116.67-100)=16.67.   
	•	(5 points) Would it be better for the producers if they were subsidized $2 per unit instead of the consumers? Why or why not? Which side of the market benefits more from subsidies?   Subsidizing the producers would be the same as subsidizing the consumers. Demand elasticity in the 2022 equilibrium is 50*10/100=5. Supply elasticity in the 2022 equilibrium is 10*10/100=1. Demand is more elastic, so consumers always benefit more regardless which side is subsidized.

	•

Problem 1 (20 points)

From the second quarter of 2020 to the fourth quarter of 2022, the median home sales price went up by 48%. This question explores a possible explanation for the rising cost of single-family houses. 

Suppose that, in 2020, the demand for houses was:

Meanwhile, the supply of houses in 2020 was: 


	•	(2 points) What is the equilibrium price and quantity of the housing market in 2020?

Price=2; quantity=100


In 2020, people started to spend a lot more time at home because of Covid. Therefore, many people wanted to scale up their houses. Moreover, the booming stock market gave a lot of cash to individual investors, causing them to have higher willingness to pay. This leads to an increase in demand. Suppose that the demand for houses in 2022 became: 


	•	(2 points) What would the equilibrium price and quantity be if the supply had stayed the same? 
Price=6; quantity=300


Moreover, the supply chain problem made it difficult and more costly for new constructions to gather materials. Social distancing and other Covid prevention measures also made any in-person work in the construction process (e.g., wiring, plumbing, and application of permits etc) difficult. These caused the supply to decrease. Suppose that the supply in 2022 became:  




	•	(4 points) What is the equilibrium price and quantity in 2022? 
Price=10; quantity=100

To make housing more affordable, suppose that the government decides to provide subsidies to home buyers. 
If there is a $1 per-unit subsidy to home buyers, what would the equilibrium price and quantity be? What is the total expenditure? What is the deadweight loss? 

	•	(5 points) If there is a $2 per-unit subsidy to home buyers, what would the equilibrium price and quantity be? What is the total expenditure? What is the deadweight loss?
Inverse demand before subsidy: 

               Inverse demand after subsidy: 
               Inverse supply: 


               Equilibrium quantity: 0.12q=14, q*=116.67. 
               Equilibrium price paid by consumers: 12-0.02*116.67=9.67.
               Equilibrium price received by producers: 11.67. 
               Total subsidy=2*116.67=233.34.
               Deadweight loss: ½*2*(116.67-100)=16.67. 


	•	(5 points) Would it be better for the producers if they were subsidized $2 per unit instead of the consumers? Why or why not? Which side of the market benefits more from subsidies? 

Subsidizing the producers would be the same as subsidizing the consumers. Demand elasticity in the 2022 equilibrium is 50*10/100=5. Supply elasticity in the 2022 equilibrium is 10*10/100=1. Demand is more elastic, so consumers always benefit more regardless which side is subsidized.

detected_equations: Profits are equal to P*Q – FC - VC * Q = 100*20/3 = 666.66667.
For business, MR = MC implies Q = 112.5 and P = 7. The profit is equal to 787.5.
For students, MR = MC implies Q = 50 and P = 5. The profit is equal to 250.

given_solution:
Problem 2 (24 points) 

CalTrain has a local monopoly in the market for railway services between Palo Alto and San Francisco. The inverse demand for rides is given by P(Q) =(40/3)-(1/15)Q, where Q is the number of tickets sold and P is the price of a ride. CalTrain incurs in variable cost of $0 per train ride and has fixed costs of zero.
	•	(6 points) Calculate the profit maximizing price and quantity for Caltrain. 



MR = MC when Q=100 and P = 20/3=6.66667..

	•	(6 points) What are the company’s profits? What is consumer surplus? What is the deadweight loss?

Profits are equal to P*Q – FC - VC * Q = 100*20/3 = 666.66667.

The consumer surplus is the area between the demand curve and the monopoly price. The base equals monopoly demand, i.e., 100. The height is the difference between the intercept of the inverse demand curve (40/3) and the price (20/3), i.e., 20/3. The area is thus 0.5*100*20/3=333.333.

The deadweight loss can be found as the area of the triangle with height the difference between price and marginal cost (20/3-0) and base the difference in the monopoly quantity (100) and the quantity under perfect competition. To find the latter we can just equate demand to supply, i.e., marginal cost): 40/3-1/15Q=0, which implies Q = 200. Hence, the area of the deadweight loss triangle is (200-100)*20/3 = 666.67.
  
[Note: Give half credit to students who identify the areas graphically but do not calculate them.]


	•	(6 points) Suppose that, upon further market research, CalTrain finds out that they are serving two distinct groups of customers: Business, with an inverse demand of P(Q) = 15-(1/15)Q, and Students, with an inverse demand of P(Q) = 10-0.1Q. If CalTrain can price-discriminate, what would be the prices offered to the two groups of customers? What is the total number of tickets sold? What is the profit?

We can treat the two markets as two separate monopolistic markets.

For business, MR = MC implies Q = 112.5 and P = 7. The profit is equal to 787.5.

For students, MR = MC implies Q = 50 and P = 5. The profit is equal to 250.

With price discrimination, total profits equal 1037.5.

	•	(2 points) Given your answers to points a), b), and c), should CalTrain price-discriminate or not? Explain.

Yes, since profits are larger in d).

	•	(4 points) Suppose CalTrain is now able to engage in perfect price discrimination. Considering the total inverse demand of point (a), what would be the company’s profits and what would be consumer surplus?

Under perfect price discrimination, consumer surplus is zero and profits correspond to the area of the triangle below the demand curve and above marginal cost. Since total quantity sold would be where demand equals supply, the base of the triangle is 200. The height of the triangle is the intercept between the intercept of the inverse demand curve (40/3) and marginal costs (0), i.e., 40/3. Total profits would thus be 1333.33. Note that this is also the sum of the deadweight loss, profit, and consumer surplus in point b).

detected_equations: They would pick the P4P job (utility = expected value, so they pick the job with the highest expected value).
• (3 points) Would your answer change if your friend is risk-averse, with a utility function u(x)= ?
The setup is identical to what we have seen in class, with S=0.7, n=0.3, c=30,000, and m = 105,000.
The optimal bonus is b* = c/(S-n) = 30,000/0.4 = $75,000

given_solution:
Problem 3 (21 points) 

A friend is deciding between two job offers: one from firm Flat, which pays a flat wage of $105,000, and one from firm P4P, which pays a base salary of $85,000 and a bonus of $35,000 upon reaching a performance target. Suppose your friend assigns a 40% probability to reaching the performance target.

	•	(2 points) What is the expected value of the two jobs to your friend? 

Flat: $105,000
P4P: $85,000 + 0.4 * 35,000 = $99,000

	•	(2 points) If your friend is risk-neutral, what job would they pick?

They would pick the P4P job (utility = expected value, so they pick the job with the highest expected value).

	•	(3 points) Would your answer change if your friend is risk-averse, with a utility function u(x)=  ?

Now utility is not equal to expected value anymore. 
Expected utility of Flat: 324.04
Expected utility of P2P: 0.6313.49

They would pick the Flat job.
Suppose now that you are a HR manager at firm P4P and are deciding whether to change your compensation scheme. Namely, you are considering adjusting your base salary and bonus to incentivize employees to work hard. Suppose your employees can choose two levels of effort: “low”, which gives them a chance equal to 30% of meeting the performance target, and “high”, which gives them a chance equal to 70%. The cost of effort is equal to $30,000. Assume, as above, that your main competitor – firm Flat – is paying employees a flat wage of $105,000.

	•	(6 points) Assuming all employees are risk-neutral, find the optimal base salary and bonus you would have to pay if you wanted to implement the bonus scheme.

The setup is identical to what we have seen in class, with S=0.7, n=0.3, c=30,000, and m = 105,000.

The optimal bonus is b* = c/(S-n) = 30,000/0.4 = $75,000

The optimal base salary is w = m – n*b = 105,000 – 0.3*75,000 = $82,500


	•	(2 points) Would your answers to point d) change if you found out your employees where risk averse? Why?

The answer to point d) assumes that workers are risk averse. If workers are risk-neutral, they would have to be compensated for the risk involved in the bonus scheme, so at least one between w and b would have to be higher.
	•	(6 points) Suppose that, upon further research, you find out that the profit margins from meeting the performance target is $60,000 per worker (that is, each worker meeting the target increases your profits, net of all non-labor costs, by $80,000). Would it be worth it for your company to stick with the bonus scheme, or would it be better off to switch to the flat wage scheme? (For this point, assume again workers are risk-neutral). Explain.

We saw in class that the per-worker, per-unit of output (in this case, the performance target) profit margin would have to be at least as great as the bonus in order to justify the bonus scheme being used. Given that we found a bonus of $75,000 in point d), a margin of $80,000 is high enough and P4P is better off offering the the bonus scheme.

given_solution:
Problem 4: Price Competition and Capacity [20 points]
Consider a one-shot price competition between Apple and Samsung for their new smartphones. Each company can chose to charge either $800 or $1,000. The following is the DEMAND matrix (not the profit matrix):
                                        Samsung
 Apple

$800
$1000

$800
30, 20
35, 10

$1000
18, 22
21, 18

For example, if both firms set a price of $800, Apple will have a demand of 30 million units and Samsung will have a demand of 20 million units. (While the demand numbers are in Millions, you don’t need to account for that in your calculations.) Suppose the production cost per unit is $400, the same for both companies. (We don’t consider fixed costs as they usually don’t impact price competition.) Assume each company aims to maximize its own profits.
a) Suppose first that the two companies set their prices simultaneously. Please answer the following questions. [Hint: Start by constructing the game matrix with profit numbers. Don’t forget to include costs, and be sure to double check your calculations before proceeding.]
(a-1) Does Samsung have a dominant strategy? If so, please specify. [4 points]
(a-2) What prices will the two companies set in the Nash Equilibrium? [4 points]

b) Suppose now that Apple announces its price first, and then Samsung responds. Please answer the following questions. [Hint: Start by drawing the game tree.]
(b-1) What prices will the two companies set? [4 points]
(b-2) If Samsung announces its price first, how will each company’s profit change? [4 points] 
c) Return to the simultaneous-move game, but now suppose Apple has a capacity constraint of 20 million units, while Samsung does not. In other words, Apple cannot serve more than 20 million customers, and any unserved customers will buy from Samsung. Please answer the following questions. [Hint: You need to re-draw a game matrix.]
(c-1) What prices will the two companies now set in the Nash Equilibrium? [2 points]
(c-2) Compared to the situation in question (a), does Apple suffer or benefit from its capacity constraint, and by how much? What about Samsung? [2 points]
