   6. [20 points] A game of add-on pricing

Consider a price competition between two movie theaters. They sell both movie tickets and popcorn.
Suppose they are offering the same film and the same type of popcorn. The cost of each movie ticket is
$20, and consumers’ willingness to pay is $30. The cost of each tub of popcorn is $2 and consumers’
willingness to pay is $8. Suppose the movie ticket price is publically observable but the popcorn price is
not (which is usually the case in the market). As a result, when consumers choose which theater to go,
they compare the ticket price only and always choose the cheaper one, but forget to take into account that
they will also buy popcorn once they arrive at a theater as long as its price does not exceed their
willingness to pay. We call such consumers “naïve consumers”. Suppose each consumer will buy at most
one tub of popcorn. Suppose the number of consumers is not big, so there is no need to worry about
capacity constraint. Suppose the two movie theaters choose their prices for the movie ticket and popcorn
simultaneously (and only once). If a consumer is indifferent between the two movie theaters, she will
randomly pick one.

              a)   [8 points] What is the price of popcorn in the Nash equilibrium? What is the
                   price of the movie ticket in the Nash equilibrium? Explain your answers.

              b)    [7 points] Suppose now that all the consumers become “savvy”: they find out
                   the popcorn prices and take them into account when they choose which movie
                   theater to go. Then what prices will the movie theaters set for their tickets and
                   popcorn, respectively, in the Nash equilibrium? What about if only half of the
                   consumers become “savvy”? Explain your answers.

              c)   [5 points] Return to the situation where all consumers are naïve. Suppose the
                   city now tries to protect consumers by setting a price cap $6 for popcorn. Will
                   consumers benefit from this intervention? Explain your answer.

1) Price will go up but not to the extent of 10 euros
   Quantities will go down
   Consumer welfare will go down

2) If the demand curve is very flat, which seems likely given the substitution possibilities, movements
   in the supply curve do little to change prices.

3) Possible explanations: (1) Economic profits can be zero while accounting profits can still be
   positive. Financial statements show accounting profits – so ignores opportunity costs.

    (2) When firms have economies of scale (e.g., when the ATC is U shaped), in the short run firms
    can earn a positive profit.

    (3) Alternatively, marginal firms in a competitive market makes zero economic profit, but this firm
    is more efficient than the marginal ones.

4) Addresses moral hazard.
   Seeks to prevent managers from taking excessive risks or engaging in fraud that may be difficult to
   observe in the short run, but become apparent in the long run.

5) A monopolist never produces on the inelastic part of his or her demand curve.
   At an inelastic point the firm can increase price without losing many customers and thus increasing
   revenue. Or mentioning that the MR goes negative when the elasticity is < absolute value of 1, or
   being on the top half of a linear demand curve.


6) Demand is less elastic in short run.
   Takes time to change behavior (example: Gasoline).
   [It is also ok if they say the opposite is true for some durable goods.]

7) Accept either               or            (make sure subscripts are correct).
    When this relationship holds, the firm has equalized the “return on investment” or “bang for the
    buck” from its purchase of inputs.

8) This is intertemporal price discrimination (a special case of 2nd degree price discrimination) or self-
    selection. Publisher wants to separate two groups: the ones who are willing to spend money to read
    the book immediately and get the hardbound edition for their libraries, and the ones who just want
    to read the book (more price sensitive).
    Without an interval, some from the first group may be tempted to buy a paperback edition and save
    some dollars.

5.   [23 points] Market for insurance


 Consider the following simple model of health insurance. Let there be three types of people, LOW,
 MEDIUM, and HIGH risk, whose yearly medical expenses are $0 with 95% probability and $25K,
 $50K, and $100K (respectively) with 5% probability for the LOW, MEDIUM and HIGH groups. All
 three risk types are equally represented in the population, and all people have an initial wealth level of
 $100K, and a utility function over wealth of u(x) = √x (where x is measured in thousands of dollars).


 Insurers only offer the simplest policy, where people pay a premium but no deductible and medical
 expenditure is fully reimbursed. Assume that insurers have no costs other than the expected payouts
 to their policyholders.


               a)   [9 points] Compute the expected cost to the insurer for each type of customer.
                    Also, compute the highest premium that each type of consumer is willing to pay:
                    You can simply copy the following table and fill it out.

  MGT 404 2017                                                                                         Final Exam




               Customer                  Expected cost of                    Highest premium
               risk type             customer type to insurer               consumer would pay
              LOW
              MEDIUM
              HIGH


The highest premium a person is willing to pay, 𝑝 (where 𝑙 is the loss in the bad state of the world) is the
solution to 5% × √100 − 𝑙 + 95% × √100 = √100 − 𝑝 . Expected cost to the insurer is 5% × 𝑙.
Filling out the table:

                             Customer      Expected cost to           Highest premium consumer
                             risk type     insurer                    would pay

                             LOW           $1250                      $1335

                             MEDIUM        $2500                      $2907

                             HIGH          $5000                      $9750

[1 point for each number in the cost column; 2 points for each number in the premium column]

                 b)   [4 points] If it were required that all people be insured, and insurance is
                      competitive so that premiums equal average cost, what would the premium be?

The average cost is (1/3)*($1250+$2500+$5000)=$2917. This is the actuarially fair premium.


                 c)   [10 points] If insurance were optional, and again insurance is competitive so that
                      premiums equal average cost, what premium would insurers charge? And which
                      risk types would choose to buy insurance at that premium? (Hint: Note that they
                      would consider charging one of the values solved for in part a. If they charged
                      the lowest, all three types would buy; if they charge the 2nd lowest, two types
                      would buy; if they charged the highest, only one type would buy).

        If the insurer prices at $1335 or below, its average cost will be
        (1/3)*($1250+$2500+$5000)=$2917. Negative profit.

        If the insurer prices between $1336 and $2907, its average cost will be
        (1/2)*($2500+$5000)=$3750. Negative profit.

        If the insurer prices between $2908 and $9750, its average cost will be $5000.

So it can avoid negative profit if it serves HIGH types only, at an actuarially fair premium of $5000.

If the student only says that only HIGH types will be served at a price of $5000, with no more
explanation, 5 points.

If they are able to rule out one of the incorrect options (but don’t get the full solution), give 3 points

   6. [20 points] A game of add-on pricing

Consider a price competition between two movie theaters. They sell both movie tickets and popcorn.
Suppose they are offering the same film and the same type of popcorn. The cost of each movie ticket is
$20, and consumers’ willingness to pay is $30. The cost of each tub of popcorn is $2 and consumers’
willingness to pay is $8. Suppose the movie ticket price is publically observable but the popcorn price is
not (which is usually the case in the market). As a result, when consumers choose which theater to go,
they compare the ticket price only and always choose the cheaper one, but forget to take into account that
they will also buy popcorn once they arrive at a theater as long as its price does not exceed their
willingness to pay. We call such consumers “naïve consumers”. Suppose each consumer will buy at most
one tub of popcorn. Suppose the number of consumers is not big, so there is no need to worry about
capacity constraint. Suppose the two movie theaters choose their prices for the movie ticket and popcorn
simultaneously (and only once). If a consumer is indifferent between the two movie theaters, she will
randomly pick one.

                 a)   [8 points] What is the price of popcorn in the Nash equilibrium? What is the
                      price of the movie ticket in the Nash equilibrium? Explain your answers.?


        Since the price of popcorn does not affect consumers’ choice of where to go, popcorn will be sold
        at the monopoly price $8. [4 points]
        Ticket will be sold at a subsidized price $14 (=20-6), where 6 is the profit from selling the
        popcorn. That is, the profit from popcorn will be competed away in attracting consumers by
        setting a low ticket price. [4 points]

                 b)   [7 points] Suppose now that all the consumers become “savvy”: they find out the
                      popcorn prices and take them into account when they choose which movie
                      theater to go. Then what prices will the movie theaters set for their tickets and
                      popcorn, respectively, in the Nash equilibrium? What about if only half of the
                      consumers become “savvy”?

        If both prices are observable, one NE is $2 for popcorn and $20 for the ticket like in the standard
        Bertrand competition. [4 points. There are many other NE: any price combination with
        $x+$y=$22 is a NE. Give full credits for any right NE.]

        If only half of the consumers are savvy, then NE is $8 for popcorn and $14 for the ticket, the
        same as in question a). [3 points]
        [Here is the reason (but this is not needed for full credits): Savvy consumers will compare the
        bundle price, but the theaters are already making zero profit, so they cannot offer better prices.

problem_text:
Problem 1:  United
At a price of $800, United would sell:

given_solution:
Problem 1. Boycotts

Note: This problem has been a tradition at Yale SOM for many years. It is a rite of passage for SOM
economics students. To answer this problem requires no mathematics beyond arithmetic. Read the
instructions carefully, and then feel free to talk about this problem with your fellow students. We
advise such discussions to prevent you from wasting time. In the end you must see the answer
yourself in your own “aha” moment and of course, the written answer must be your own.

Setup A frequently used instrument of economic coercion is the boycott. For example, a union
involved in a serious and protracted conflict with some employer or group of employers may urge
all of its sympathizers to stop buying the products of that employer or group, in the hope that the
resulting economic losses will provide an incentive for the employers to make concessions.
However, experience shows that the coercive power of a boycott is often small.

In this exercise, you are to use supply and demand analysis as a tool to understand the effects of
boycotts. For the sake of concreteness and precision, most of the questions are posed in
quantitative terms. However, the goal of the exercise is to develop your understanding of the logic
of the market processes involved. Accordingly, if you have difficulty, don't try to overwhelm the
problem with calculation -- rethink the economics.

Throughout the exercise, the following assumptions are to be maintained. First, the market in
question is one to which simple supply and demand analysis is applicable: there are numerous
buyers and sellers in the market; the good is homogeneous; buyers and sellers seek only to trade on
the most advantageous possible terms and, aside from the complications introduced by the boycott,
do not care with whom they transact. Second, the identities of buyers and sellers are fixed: in
particular, no reselling of the goods takes place. Third, the set of buyers divides sharply into
observers and nonobservers of the boycott, while the sellers separate equally sharply into those
that are targets of the boycott and those that are not. Boycott observers will not, under any
circumstances, buy from a target seller, while non-observers do not care from whom they buy. For
simplicity, it is assumed that boycott observers account for the same fraction of demand at every
price. Similarly, target sellers account for a certain fraction of the supply at every price.

    Assume initially that in the pre-boycott situation, the market demand curve is described by:

                                           𝑄   = 5000 − 10𝑃

where 𝑄 = quantity demanded and P = the price. The market supply curve has equation (with 𝑄 =
quantity supplied):

                                           𝑄 = 50 (𝑃 − 5)

except that, at prices of 5 or less, quantity supplied is zero.

Warmup problem (i): Determine the market equilibrium in the pre-boycott situation.
 
    Answer: In equilibrium, the price is such that quantity demanded equals quantity supplied:

                                     𝑄 = 𝑄
                                     5000 – 10𝑃 = 50 (𝑃 − 5)
                                     60𝑃 = 5250
                                     𝑃 = 87.5
                                     𝑄 = 4125




Warmup problem (ii): Suppose that buyers representing 70% of the original demand, observe the
boycott. If all non-observers buy from target sellers, what is the demand curve facing target sellers?


       Answer: Since observers will not buy from target sellers at any price, the only demand facing
       target sellers is from non-observers. Since observers and non-observers account for
       demand in the ratio 0.7:0.3 at every price, the equation of the demand curve facing target
       sellers is:

                              𝑄   = 0.3 (5000 − 10𝑃) = 1500 − 3𝑃



Questions
Consider the three situations characterized by the following table

                               Observers as Fraction           Target as Fraction
       Situation               of Original Demand              of Original Supply

         A                             3/4                             3/4

         B                             1/4                             1/4

         C                             1/2                             1/4

For each of the three situations, determine the market equilibrium that would arise under boycott
conditions. Give the quantities transacted by observing and non-observing buyers, and by target
and non-target sellers.

Drawing on your results, state the general principle which relates the effectiveness of a boycott to
the observance fraction and the target fraction. “Effectiveness,” in this context, means lowering the
price to target sellers. Can you provide some intuition as well?

Hint: Warmup problem (i) supplies all the mathematical knowledge you will need to solve the
remaining problems. What is needed for the rest is an ability to structure the problem, and an
understanding of simple economic principles. The point of the exercise is to determine whether the
boycott is effective in any of the three cases above. To answer this question, you have been given
 
the following information: (1) the market supply curve, (2) the targeted fraction of sellers, (3) the
market demand curve, (4) the observers as a fraction of buyers. Items (1) and (2) imply that it is
possible to write down algebraic expressions for the supply curves of targeted and non-targeted
sellers. You can also choose to use Excel to get a sense of how prices get determined in each sub-
market.
problem_text:
Problem 1. Boycotts 
Note: This problem has been a tradition at Yale SOM for many years. It is a rite of passage for SOM economics students. To answer this problem requires no mathematics beyond arithmetic. Read the instructions carefully, and then feel free to talk about this problem with your fellow students. We advise such discussions to prevent you from wasting time. In the end you must see the answer yourself in your own “aha” moment and of course, the written answer must be your own. 
Setup A frequently used instrument of economic coercion is the boycott. For example, a union involved in a serious and protracted conflict with some employer or group of employers may urge all of its sympathizers to stop buying the products of that employer or group, in the hope that the resulting economic losses will provide an incentive for the employers to make concessions. However, experience shows that the coercive power of a boycott is often small. 
In this exercise, you are to use supply and demand analysis as a tool to understand the effects of boycotts. For the sake of concreteness and precision, most of the questions are posed in quantitative terms. However, the goal of the exercise is to develop your understanding of the logic of the market processes involved. Accordingly, if you have difficulty, don't try to overwhelm the problem with calculation -- rethink the economics. 
Throughout the exercise, the following assumptions are to be maintained. First, the market in question is one to which simple supply and demand analysis is applicable: there are numerous buyers and sellers in the market; the good is homogeneous; buyers and sellers seek only to trade on the most advantageous possible terms and, aside from the complications introduced by the boycott, do not care with whom they transact. Second, the identities of buyers and sellers are fixed: in particular, no reselling of the goods takes place. Third, the set of buyers divides sharply into observers and nonobservers of the boycott, while the sellers separate equally sharply into those that are targets of the boycott and those that are not. Boycott observers will not, under any circumstances, buy from a target seller, while non-observers do not care from whom they buy. For simplicity, it is assumed that boycott observers account for the same fraction of demand at every price. Similarly, target sellers account for a certain fraction of the supply at every price. 
Assume initially that in the pre-boycott situation, the market demand curve is described by: 

where  = quantity demanded and P = the price. The market supply curve has equation (with  = quantity supplied): 

except that, at prices of 5 or less, quantity supplied is zero. 
Warmup problem (i): Determine the market equilibrium in the pre-boycott situation. 

Warmup problem (ii): Suppose that buyers representing 70% of the original demand, observe the boycott. If all non-observers buy from target sellers, what is the demand curve facing target sellers? 


Questions
Consider the three situations characterized by the following table

Observers as Fraction	Target as Fraction
Situation		of Original Demand		of Original Supply 

   A				3/4				 3/4

   B				1/4				 1/4

   C				1/2				 1/4

For each of the three situations, determine the market equilibrium that would arise under boycott conditions.  Give the quantities transacted by observing and non-observing buyers, and by target and non-target sellers.

Drawing on your results, state the general principle which relates the effectiveness of a boycott to the observance fraction and the target fraction.  “Effectiveness,” in this context, means lowering the price to target sellers.  Can you provide some intuition as well?

Hint: Warmup problem (i) supplies all the mathematical knowledge you will need to solve the remaining problems.  What is needed for the rest is an ability to structure the problem, and an understanding of simple economic principles.  The point of the exercise is to determine whether the boycott is effective in any of the three cases above.  To answer this question, you have been given the following information: (1) the market supply curve, (2) the targeted fraction of sellers, (3) the market demand curve, (4) the observers as a fraction of buyers.  Items (1) and (2) imply that it is possible to write down algebraic expressions for the supply curves of targeted and non-targeted sellers.  You can also choose to use Excel to get a sense of how prices get determined in each sub-market.

given_solution:
Problem 2. Black quinoa in Bolivia

The Government of Bolivia is planning to impose a price control in the market for black quinoa, an
important part of the standard diet of people in the country. Market demand for black quinoa is
given by 𝑄 = 40 – 𝑃 and the market supply curve is given by 𝑄 = −20 + 3𝑃, where P denotes
the market price in dollars per kilogram and 𝑄 and 𝑄 denote the quantities demanded and
supplied, respectively (measured in millions of kilograms per year).

   a. What is the equilibrium market price and quantity? What is the consumer surplus and the
      producer surplus?

           a. Answer: 40-P=-20+3P

               P=15

               So, Q=25

               C.S.=(1/2)*25*25=312.25

               P.S.=(1/2)*25*(25/3)=104.17



   b. Find the elasticity of demand at the equilibrium price? Is the demand for quinoa elastic or
      inelastic? Is this elasticity consistent with what you expected? Explain

           a. Answer: Elasticity= -15/25=-0.6

               So demand is inelastic which is consistent with quinoa being an essential part of the
               Bolivian diet.



   c. Consider the situation where the Government imposes a price control limiting market price
      to no higher than $10. What is the new equilibrium price and quantity? What are the
      resulting consumer surplus, producer surplus and total surplus?

           a. Answer: New price is $10

               New quantity is 10

               C.S=(1/2)*10*10+20*10=250

               P.S.=(1/2)*10*(10/3)=16.67

               Total Surplus=266.67
 
d. Looking at the effect of the price control on the total surplus, what would you say? Is this an
   efficient step? Why or why not?

       a. Answer: This was not an efficient step since total social surplus went down.



e. What if the government limited the market price to $15.50? Would this be a smart move?
   Why or why not?

       a. Answer: A price control of $15.50 would not have any effect since the equilibrium
          price is already below $15.50.

Problem 1. Tariffs and Import quotas on vegetable fiber

a.      Consider the market for vegetable fiber in the US. The domestic demand for vegetable fiber
is given by

                                          𝑄 = 40 − 2𝑃
       where 𝑄 is quantity measured in millions of pounds and P is the price per pound.
       Vegetable fiber is also produced domestically in the US and the domestic supply curve is
       given by
                                                 2
                                            𝑄 = 𝑃
                                                 3
       where 𝑄 is US output in millions of pounds and P is the domestic price per pound.

       Solve for the domestic (autarky) equilibrium price and quantity of vegetable fiber.

       Price: _____________________

       Quantity: _____________________


       Solution: 40-2P=2/3P P=15 and Q =10.


   b. Vegetable fiber trades in a highly competitive world market at a price of $9 per pound.
       Suppose the US opens up to free trade and there is enough availability that the US can
       import as much as it needs at this price. Do you think US would import vegetable fiber? If so,
       write the quantity that will be imported. If not, enter “0”.

       Quantity imported: ________________________



       Solution: Demand would be 22, supply would be 6, so 16 would be imported.


   c. Subsequently, the US government imposed a $9 import tariff. Under this tariff, what do you
      predict is the domestic price of vegetable fiber?


       US domestic price in regime with import tariffs: _____________________

       Solution: This would raise the effective world price to $18, and the US would no longer
       import any fiber. The price would be the autarky price of 15. (They would not become an
       exporter, as the true world price remains at 9).


   d. What would be the total tariff revenue collected by the Government?

       Tariff revenue: ________________________

       Solution: 0, since none is imported.
 
e. Without any further computations, can you predict who would be the gainers and losers
   from such a tariff?

     Relative to free trade, US domestic producer surplus under the import would …

     □ Increase

     □ Decrease

     □ Unclear without more information


     US domestic consumer surplus (under the tariff vs free trade) would …

     □ Increase

     □ Decrease

     □ Unclear without more information


     Total US domestic surplus (under the tariff vs free trade) would …

     □ Increase

     □ Decrease

     □ Unclear without more information


     Why did you conclude this about total surplus? ___________________________________________

     __________________________________________________________________________________________________

     __________________________________________________________________________________________________

     Answer. US domestic producers would be better off under the tariff, US consumers would be
     worse off, and total surplus would unambiguously decrease as the tariff would introduce
     deadweight loss.


f.   (Challenge question, diagram helps here): Under pressure from trading partners, the US is
     considering elimination of the tariff. In its place, the proposal is to limit imports into the US
     to 8 million pounds a year (𝑄 = 8). Under this proposal, what would you predict to be the
     equilibrium domestic price of vegetable fiber (understanding that the quota would lead to
     upward price pressure if the quota is binding)? What will be the amount produced by
     domestic producers?


     Answer.

     To figure out domestic price, note that demand must equal supply, and the supply will be
     met partly by domestic producers and partly by world producers (up to 8 million pounds).
 
        So in equilibrium under this export quantity constraint, we will have domestic demand
        outstrip domestic supply by 8 million pounds.

        Domestic price: _____________________

        Quantity produced by domestic suppliers: _____________________


        40-2P=8+2/3PP=12

        Domestic Q=8

Problem 2. Hard Seltzer

The latest craze among MBA students is “hard seltzer” drinks such as White Claw and Truly that are
lightly flavored and low-calorie alcoholic beverages. An enterprising SOM alum is hoping to get in
on the fast-growing market by producing and selling her own hard seltzer, “Blue Paw,” in the New
Haven area.

She has secured a vacant former brewery and faces monthly fixed costs of $2,500. She estimates her
variable costs, as a function of the quantity of cases of Blue Paw produced 𝑞, is given by:

                                        𝑉𝐶(𝑞) = 2.5𝑞 + 0.01𝑞

    a) Suppose the prevailing price in the New Haven area for case of hard seltzer is $15. How
       many cans will she choose to produce in a month? What are her monthly profits?

Quantity supplied: ________________

Monthly Profits: ________________

Solution: Take the derivative of VC to get MC; this is the firm’s supply curve. Set P=15:

                            𝑀𝐶(𝑞) = 2.5 + 0.02𝑞
                            15 = 2.5 + 0.02𝑞
                            𝑞 = 625
                            𝜋 = 15 ∗ 625 − 2500 − 2.5 ∗ 625 − 0.01(625 )
                            = 1,406.25

    b) The popularity of these drinks has attracted a large number of entrants to the hard seltzer
       market, putting downward pressure on price. The entrepreneur has noticed that all of the
       entrants are offering very similar products and have similar production capabilities to Blue
       Paw. At what prevailing price for a case of hard seltzer would she stop producing in the
       short run?

Solution: look at the min(AVC), a price of $2.50 per case.
 
   c) The market for the drinks stabilizes over the long run as the market matures. What do you
      expect the long run price to be for a case of hard seltzer to be?

Solution: look at min(ATC), where ATC equals MC:

                                                2500
                               2.5 + 0.02𝑞 =         + 2.5 + 0.01𝑞
                                                 𝑞
                                         2500
                               0.01𝑞 =
                                          𝑞
                               𝑞 = 500
                               𝑝 = 12.5

Problem 1. Tariffs and Import quotas on vegetable fiber

a.      Consider the market for vegetable fiber in the US. The domestic demand for vegetable fiber
is given by

                                          𝑄 = 40 − 2𝑃
       where 𝑄 is quantity measured in millions of pounds and P is the price per pound.
       Vegetable fiber is also produced domestically in the US and the domestic supply curve is
       given by
                                                 2
                                            𝑄 = 𝑃
                                                 3
       where 𝑄 is US output in millions of pounds and P is the domestic price per pound.

       Solve for the domestic (autarky) equilibrium price and quantity of vegetable fiber.

       Price: _____________________

       Quantity: _____________________


       Solution: 40-2P=2/3P P=15 and Q =10.


   b. Vegetable fiber trades in a highly competitive world market at a price of $9 per pound.
       Suppose the US opens up to free trade and there is enough availability that the US can
       import as much as it needs at this price. Do you think US would import vegetable fiber? If so,
       write the quantity that will be imported. If not, enter “0”.

       Quantity imported: ________________________



       Solution: Demand would be 22, supply would be 6, so 16 would be imported.


   c. Subsequently, the US government imposed a $9 import tariff. Under this tariff, what do you
      predict is the domestic price of vegetable fiber?


       US domestic price in regime with import tariffs: _____________________

       Solution: This would raise the effective world price to $18, and the US would no longer
       import any fiber. The price would be the autarky price of 15. (They would not become an
       exporter, as the true world price remains at 9).


   d. What would be the total tariff revenue collected by the Government?

       Tariff revenue: ________________________

       Solution: 0, since none is imported.
 
e. Without any further computations, can you predict who would be the gainers and losers
   from such a tariff?

     Relative to free trade, US domestic producer surplus under the import would …

     □ Increase

     □ Decrease

     □ Unclear without more information


     US domestic consumer surplus (under the tariff vs free trade) would …

     □ Increase

     □ Decrease

     □ Unclear without more information


     Total US domestic surplus (under the tariff vs free trade) would …

     □ Increase

     □ Decrease

     □ Unclear without more information


     Why did you conclude this about total surplus? ___________________________________________

     __________________________________________________________________________________________________

     __________________________________________________________________________________________________

     Answer. US domestic producers would be better off under the tariff, US consumers would be
     worse off, and total surplus would unambiguously decrease as the tariff would introduce
     deadweight loss.


f.   (Challenge question, diagram helps here): Under pressure from trading partners, the US is
     considering elimination of the tariff. In its place, the proposal is to limit imports into the US
     to 8 million pounds a year (𝑄 = 8). Under this proposal, what would you predict to be the
     equilibrium domestic price of vegetable fiber (understanding that the quota would lead to
     upward price pressure if the quota is binding)? What will be the amount produced by
     domestic producers?


     Answer.

     To figure out domestic price, note that demand must equal supply, and the supply will be
     met partly by domestic producers and partly by world producers (up to 8 million pounds).
 
        So in equilibrium under this export quantity constraint, we will have domestic demand
        outstrip domestic supply by 8 million pounds.

        Domestic price: _____________________

        Quantity produced by domestic suppliers: _____________________


        40-2P=8+2/3PP=12

        Domestic Q=8

MGT 404 2018                                                                                          Final Exam



Section I: Short Questions [70 points]
Please be concise. None of the answers require more than a brief paragraph.


1) [10 points] The Government of Malawi is planning to subsidize families that choose to take flu
   shots. Do you think this intervention would be beneficial or detrimental to society? Please
   provide a precise economic argument for your view.




2) [10 points] A study by the Haas Avocado Board notes that that there would likely be a 10%
   decrease in avocado crop because of an unusual heatwave in early July in California. The study
   further predicts that this 10% decrease in supply would drive avocado prices in the US up by as
   much as 30%, a remarkably large impact. From reading this study, what can you infer about the
   elasticity of demand for avocado - do you think demand for avocado is likely to be elastic or
   inelastic? Does this make sense intuitively? Why or why not?




3)    [10 points] A branded drug is a pharmaceutical that is covered by a patent. By law, only one
     firm can produce it, for a given length of time. After that time patents expire, and when this
     happens, a generic drug can enter the marketplace. The generic drug is chemically identical
     and is typically priced well below the price of the branded drug. It is common to observe that
     when a generic drug is introduced into the marketplace, the price of the branded (e.g.
     previously patented) drug increases. Explain why this might happen




4)    [10 points] A local company assembles machines using domestically-sourced steel and
     imported precision aluminum. They had already solved for an optimal mix of these inputs when
     the US dollar began to appreciate. As the US dollar strengthens, would you expect them to
     change their input mix towards using more steel, more aluminum, or continue with the same
     mix? Provide a formal economic reason for your answer,




5) [10 points] A television pundit claimed that while it is true that an import tariff is likely to
   increase prices to consumers, since the government collects that tariff revenue, the country is
   not any worse off. Do you agree or not, and why?

Section I: Short Questions [70 points]
Please be concise. None of the answers require more than a brief paragraph.


1) [10 points] The Government of Malawi is planning to subsidize families that choose to take flu
   shots. Do you think this intervention would be beneficial or detrimental to society? Please
   provide a precise economic argument for your view.




2) [10 points] A study by the Haas Avocado Board notes that that there would likely be a 10%
   decrease in avocado crop because of an unusual heatwave in early July in California. The study
   further predicts that this 10% decrease in supply would drive avocado prices in the US up by as
   much as 30%, a remarkably large impact. From reading this study, what can you infer about the
   elasticity of demand for avocado - do you think demand for avocado is likely to be elastic or
   inelastic? Does this make sense intuitively? Why or why not?




3)    [10 points] A branded drug is a pharmaceutical that is covered by a patent. By law, only one
     firm can produce it, for a given length of time. After that time patents expire, and when this
     happens, a generic drug can enter the marketplace. The generic drug is chemically identical
     and is typically priced well below the price of the branded drug. It is common to observe that
     when a generic drug is introduced into the marketplace, the price of the branded (e.g.
     previously patented) drug increases. Explain why this might happen




4)    [10 points] A local company assembles machines using domestically-sourced steel and
     imported precision aluminum. They had already solved for an optimal mix of these inputs when
     the US dollar began to appreciate. As the US dollar strengthens, would you expect them to
     change their input mix towards using more steel, more aluminum, or continue with the same
     mix? Provide a formal economic reason for your answer,




5) [10 points] A television pundit claimed that while it is true that an import tariff is likely to
   increase prices to consumers, since the government collects that tariff revenue, the country is
   not any worse off. Do you agree or not, and why?

 6) [10 points] Many executive compensation packages include “stock options” as part of the
    compensation scheme. What is the economic rationale for this type of compensation, as
    opposed to, say, granting shares or paying a fixed salary?




 7) [10 points] Philadelphia, the nation's largest city with a soda tax levies a tax of 1.5-cents-per-
    ounce on the manufacturers of sweetened beverages. Analysts predict that the price of a 12
    pack of 12-ounce sodas would increase by $2.16. Do you agree with this prediction? Argue
    why or why not.




                           Section II: Long Questions – Total of 110 points



   1. [20 points] Local ready-mix concrete market

Transition-Mix has a local monopoly over the ready-mix concrete industry in lower Manhattan. The
demand for ready-mix concrete is given by Q =100-2P, where Q is the quantity of ready-mix concrete in
thousand cubic yards per month, and P is the price in dollars per cubic yard. Transition-Mix incurs a fixed
cost of production of $12 and a variable cost of $5 per cubic yard.


    a) [3 points] What are the profit maximizing price and quantity choices for Transition-Mix?




    b) [3 points] What is the elasticity of demand at this price? Does this elasticity make sense to you?
       Why or why not?

   3. [20 points] Price Discrimination at Pear Inc

The high‐tech company Pear Inc. has developed an exciting new product: Myphone with a large
interactive screen and many customizable features. Pear Inc. is considering the introduction of both a
regular version which costs $200 to produce and a slightly higher‐end version with more features called
“Myphone Super” which costs $350 to produce. Pear’s marketing departments suggests that consumers
can be categorized as loyalists and casual users. Each consumer would consider at most one phone.



Consumer Type            Number of consumers      Willingness to pay for   Willingness to pay for
                                                  Myphone                  Myphone Super

Casual                   10 million               500                      600

Loyalist                 1 million                800                      1100



You have been hired to advise Pear on its pricing strategy.

    a) [15 points] Would you recommend that Pear Inc sell one particular model or both? What is the
       optimal pricing strategy that you would recommend to Pear Inc.? Consider all possible strategies
       and explain your reasoning systematically and completely.




    b) [5 points] How would your answer change, if at all, if the causal segment were willing to pay
       $675 for the Myphone Super?

   4. [15 points] Vehicle Insurance.

Vehicle insurance markets are a classic example of adverse selection. One feature of most states is a
requirement to have vehicle insurance. Did you know that in New Hampshire, you are not required to
carry insurance as a driver? Let us examine how the market performs in New Hampshire. The state has
considered mandating insurance. We will examine the market for this vehicle insurance for drivers in the
30-35 age group.

In this age group, there are two types of drivers: “Safe” types, who make up 80% of this population, and
“Risky” types, who make up the other 20%. The Safe types have expected annual accident expenses that
are uniformly distributed between $0 and $1000. The Risky types are guaranteed to have $1600 in annual
expenses. All customers know their expected annual accident expenses, but insurers do not.

Math reminder: the mean of the uniform distribution between a and b is (a+b)/2, and the probability that
a draw from such a distribution is at or above p is (b-p)/(b-a).


    a) [5 points] First, suppose hypothetically that all individuals in this market (drivers between 30 and
       35 in age) will purchase insurance plans because they are required to. We will assume the market
       is competitive, and that insurers have no cost advantages, so that premiums equal average costs.
       What will be the resulting policy premiums with all individuals covered?




    b) [5 points] In reality, consumers will purchase an insurance plan only if the premium is less than
       their expected expenses (i.e. they are risk-neutral; making them risk-averse would complicate the
       math without changing the intuition). At the price you just found, what proportion of the Safe
       types would choose to purchase vehicle insurance if the requirement to purchase were lifted?


    c) [5 points] The New Hampshire state government has proposed a “mandate,” which is a penalty
       that uninsured drivers must pay. What is the smallest penalty that will cause the market to arrive
       at the situation described in Part (a), where all consumers are purchasing the insurance.


   5. [20 points] A Voting Game

A three-person committee has to choose a winner for a national painting prize. After some debate, there
are three paintings still under consideration: a Cubist painting, an Expressionist painting, and a slightly
more traditional Impressionist painting. Let’s call these paintings C, E and I; and call the committee
members 1, 2 and 3. The preferences of the committee members are as follows:

1: C>E>I

2: I>C>E

3: E>I>C

   5. [20 points] A Voting Game

A three-person committee has to choose a winner for a national painting prize. After some debate, there
are three paintings still under consideration: a Cubist painting, an Expressionist painting, and a slightly
more traditional Impressionist painting. Let’s call these paintings C, E and I; and call the committee

given_solution:
6
 
 MGT 404 2018                                                                                          Final Exam



For example, Member 1 strictly prefers the C to E and strictly prefers E to I.

The rules of the competition say that, if they disagree, they should vote (secret ballot, one member one
vote, and the painting with the most votes wins). If the vote is tied (which happens when each painting
has one vote), the winner will be the painting for which member 1 voted. So, it seems that member 1
might have an advantage. Consider this simultaneous voting game where each member has three
strategies: C, E, or I, and answer the following questions:


[Hint: You do NOT need to know the exact payoffs. Any payoffs will be fine provided that they are
consistent with the preference orders given above. And you do NOT need to draw a matrix form.
Recall the definition of Nash equilibrium (NE) before you start.]


    a) [6 points] Is it a Nash equilibrium that each member votes for their favorite painting (i.e. (C, I,
       E))? Please explain your answer.




    b) [6 points] Is it possible that in a NE member 1’s least favorite painting (i.e., painting I) wins but
       nobody votes for their least favorite painting (which is anyway a weakly dominated strategy)? If
       yes, please write down the NE which specifies each member’s vote; if no, please explain.



    c) [4 points] This game has multiple NE. You don’t need to figure out all possible NE. But, among
       the following possible voting outcomes:
                        (C, E, I); (C, C, E); (E, I, I),
       which one(s) is Nash equilibrium?



Now suppose the three members vote in a sequential and public way with the order of 1, 2 and 3, so that
member 2 can observe member 1’s vote and member 3 can observe both 1 and 2’s votes.

    d) [4 points] Which painting will then win the prize? (You are not required to draw the game-tree.)




   6. [20 points] A Bargaining Game

Consider two players A and B. They need to divide a prize $50. The game goes as follows:

In the first round, player A proposes a division plan: x for herself and 50-x for player B. After receiving
the proposal, player B decides whether to accept it or not. If it gets accepted, they divide the prize
accordingly and the game ends. If it gets rejected, the game will continue to the second round, but the
prize will be reduced to $40.

given_solution:
7
 
 MGT 404 2018                                                                                          Final Exam



In the second round, it is player B’s turn to make the proposal: y for himself and 40-y for player A. Then
player A decides whether to accept it or not. If it gets accepted, they divide the prize accordingly and the
game ends. If it gets rejected, the game ends and both players get nothing.

Suppose each player just wants to get as much money as possible for themselves, and they DO NOT care
about anything else (e.g., fairness or the other player’s welfare).


A few extra rules (which you should read carefully):

    (i)     Consider integer numbers only.
    (ii)    Zero offer is not allowed (i.e., 1x49, 1y39).
    (iii)   When a player is indifferent between accepting and rejecting, let us suppose she or he will
            accept.

    a) [5 points] If the game continues to the second round, how much will player B propose for
       himself?




    b) [5 point] Anticipating that, how much will player A propose for herself in the first round?



Suppose now that the game potentially has three rounds. If player B’s offer in the second round is
rejected, the game will continue to the third round and the prize will be further reduced to $30. In the third
round, it is player A’s turn again to make the proposal: z for herself and 30-z for player B, where 1z29.
Then player B decides whether to accept it or not. If it gets accepted, they divide the prize accordingly;
otherwise, the game ends and both players get nothing.


    c) [5 points] How much will player A then propose for herself in the first round?



Suppose now there is an additional “fairness” constraint in any round of the bargaining game: no
player accepts an offer which gives her/him $6 or more less than the other player.

    d) [5 points] How will your answer to question (c) change?

given_solution:
S

 Section I: Short Questions [70 points]
 Please be concise. None of the answers require more than a brief paragraph.



    1) Vaccines have positive externalities – this means people underestimate the benefit of a
       vaccine and therefore tend to under-consume. A subsidy can help alleviate this distortion.

    2) If a relatively small shift in the supply curve causes a big change in prices, this indicates
        that the demand curve must be quite steep. So you would expect demand for avocado to be
        inelastic.
    This can make sense. Possible reasons: (i) The amount a household spends on avocado is likely
    very small as a proportion of total budget and so you can expect that people aren’t very price
    sensitive. (ii) Very important ingredient for many restaurants because there aren’t good
    substitutes (because it is a very popular item) – so they are less price sensitive.


    3) The availability of cheaper substitutes will make some consumers move from using the branded
       product to using the generic. The consumers who continue to buy the branded drug are the less
       price elastic consumers. The branded firm now faces a customer segment that is less price
       sensitive. This means the firm with the branded drug can raise prices.

    4) A stronger US dollar means imported aluminum is cheaper; they would move towards
       using more aluminum. The MPI of aluminum would increase, but MPI of steel stays the
       same.

    5) The tariff introduces deadweight loss; the government revenue is less than the surplus
       destroyed. Therefore, no, the country is indeed worse off. If they mention DWL, full credit.

    6) The issue is that stock options are all “upside” and no downside. These “convexify” the CEO’s
       utility function. These “correct” a risk-averse CEO’s incentives around the share price (make
       CEOs behave in the risk-neutral way that shareholders want them to, while pure share grants or
       salary don’t address risk aversion).

    7) No, the price would not go up by $2.16 (the full extent of the tax). This is because the
       incidence of tax is shared by manufacturers and consumers based on the relative elasticity
       of demand and supply. So tax will be partly borne by manufacturers too and price will go
       up by less than $2.16




                         Section II: Long Questions – Total of 110 points

       a)

       But now Strategy 1 gives a higher profit. So Pear Inc should just sell Myphone Super at $675.
       This gives profit of (675-350)x11 million= 3.575 billion.

In a competitive market, the premiums will equal the average cost of $720.

We know that a consumer will buy the policy if their expected expense is greater than the premium. For
safe drivers, the proportion that will buy the policy at $320 is:



                                              $1000 − $720
                                          =                = 0.28
                                                 $1000



So only 28% of safe drivers will buy the plan when the premium is $720.

    Right away, we see the challenge without universal coverage: without some way of forcing
    individuals to buy insurance, the safest drivers in a market would choose not to participate, which
    would increase the average cost, which would increase prices, and so on.

    win, but that’s the worst outcome for member 1.

    Therefore, E will win.
    If member 2 votes for I, I will win as in the first case.
    As a result, in this sequential voting game, painting E will win.

given_solution:
Problem 7: The game of hungry lions (15 points)

A lamb has just been thrown to four hungry lions. The lions are chained down in a line, with Lion 1
closest to the lamb and Lion 4 farthest from the lamb. Each lion’s chain is short enough that it can only
reach the two players immediately adjacent to it. The game proceeds as follows. Lion 1 first decides
whether or not to eat the lamb, and then lion 2 decides whether or not to eat Lion 1, and so on. If a lion

                                                     10
 
                                                                                                           Final Exam



eats anything, it will be then too heavy to defend itself so become vulnerable to attack. If a lion remains
hungry, the next lion will not attack it because a fight of two hungry lions would kill both. Each lion’s
preferences are fairly natural: best is to eat and stay alive, next best is to stay live but go hungry, next is to
eat and be eaten, and worst is to go hungry and be eaten (which wouldn’t happen here).



    a) (5 points) Will the lamb get eaten? Please explain.
       No, the lamb will survive. [2 points]
       Backward induction: L4 will eat if L3 has eaten; anticipating that, L3 won’t eat even if L2 has
       eaten; anticipating that, L2 will eat if L1 has eaten; then L1 shouldn’t eat in the beginning. [3
       points]

    b) (5 points) If there are 5 lions, will your answer to (a) change? Please explain.
       Now the lamb will get eaten. [2 points]
       The same backward induction: now L2 won’t eat even if L1 has eaten, so anticipating that L1 will
       eat the lamb. [3 points]

    c) (5 points) If there are 𝑁 lions in general (where 𝑁 is a positive integer), will the lamb get eaten?
       Please explain.
       It depends on whether N is an odd number or an even number. When it is an even number, we
       have the same result as in (a) and the lamb will survive. When it is an odd number, we have the
       same result as in (b) and the lamb will get eaten. [full points once they point out whether N is
       even or odd matters.]




                                                       11

Problem 7: The game of hungry lions (15 points)
A lamb has just been thrown to four hungry lions. The lions are chained down in a line, with Lion 1 closest to the lamb and Lion 4 farthest from the lamb. Each lion’s chain is short enough that it can only reach the two players immediately adjacent to it. The game proceeds as follows. Lion 1 first decides whether or not to eat the lamb, and then lion 2 decides whether or not to eat Lion 1, and so on. If a lion eats anything, it will be then too heavy to defend itself so become vulnerable to attack. If a lion remains hungry, the next lion will not attack it because a fight of two hungry lions would kill both. Each lion’s preferences are fairly natural: best is to eat and stay alive, next best is to stay live but go hungry, next is to eat and be eaten, and worst is to go hungry and be eaten (which wouldn’t happen here).

	•	(5 points) Will the lamb get eaten? Please explain. 
No, the lamb will survive. 
Backward induction: L4 will eat if L3 has eaten; anticipating that, L3 won’t eat even if L2 has eaten; anticipating that, L2 will eat if L1 has eaten; then L1 shouldn’t eat in the beginning. 

	•	(5 points) If there are 5 lions, will your answer to (a) change? Please explain. 
Now the lamb will get eaten. 
The same backward induction: now L2 won’t eat even if L1 has eaten, so anticipating that L1 will eat the lamb. 

	•	(5 points) If there are  lions in general (where  is a positive integer), will the lamb get eaten? Please explain. 
It depends on whether N is an odd number or an even number. When it is an even number, we have the same result as in (a) and the lamb will survive. When it is an odd number, we have the same result as in (b) and the lamb will get eaten. [full points once they point out whether N is even or odd matters.]

       status and assumed that everyone, families and students alike, would sign up as a student? (i.e.
       because all families have at least one member with a .edu email address)

        Total market demand
        Q= 880-8P if P <50 and Q=630-3P if P>50

        Now using aggregate demand, we have
        P=110-(1/8)Q
        MR=110-(1/4)Q
        MR=MC yields Q=320 and P=70.

        But notice that 70 is above the price at which any student would buy.
        So the correct price to set in this case is still $120. – the profit maximizing price for the family
        segment.

subparts.label: c
subparts.text: c) (4 points) How much would Amazon have been willing to pay for the verification system?

Problem 3: Bars in New Haven (16 points)

New Haven has a lively bar scene in the downtown core, with creatively named places like “BAR”
providing pizza and drinks to the town’s residents. Unfortunately, things have been getting a little extra
rowdy lately, and the town would like to tax alcohol consumption in the downtown core to deter things
from getting out of hand.

        The supply and demand of alcoholic beverages in downtown New Haven are given by:
                                            𝑄 = 400𝑃 − 1000
                                            𝑄 = 3000 − 400𝑃


                                                       5


                                                                                                  Final Exam




       a) (4 points) What is the market equilibrium price and quantity prior to the imposition of any
          tax?

       b) (4 points) Suppose the tax is $1 per beverage. What is the new equilibrium price and
          quantity?

       c) (5 points) The town alders have some concerns about this proposed tax. They want to be
          certain that it i) reduces consumption by at least 10% to solve the rowdiness problem, but also
          that ii) consumer surplus falls by less than 30% to ensure they are re-elected. Would this tax
          meet both criteria i and ii?

          surplus?

Problem 4: Mac Pro Assemby (16 points)


Apple assembles its made-in-the-USA “Mac Pro” product at a facility in Texas using a mix of labor (“l”)
and machines (“m”). They have the following production function for each assembly line, where output is
in 1000s of units per year:

                                  𝑓(𝑚, 𝑙) = 2 ⋅ 𝑚 + 8 ⋅ 𝑙 + 0.2 ⋅ 𝑚 ⋅ 𝑙


                                                   6


                                                                                                   Final Exam




They page workers a salary and benefits package that costs $100K per year, while machines cost $50K
per year in maintenance and licensing fees from their manufacturer.

    a) (6 points) Apple is currently employs 15 people and 32 machines on its first assembly line. What
       is the output of the assembly line per year, and how much does Apple spend to produce that
       output?

    b) (4 points) Apple wants to know if this is the cost-minimizing production plan. Compute the
       marginal productivity of investing (MPI) of each input (where MPI is the marginal product of the
       input scaled by the input price, in thousands). Could Apple do better?

    c) (6 points) Based on the production function and current input prices, can you come up with a
       general rule of how many workers Apple should employ, as a function of the number of machines
       it uses? In other words, I am asking for the optimal 𝑙 ∗ as a function of 𝑚∗ .

Problem 1  (15 points)

Google has introduced a new product known as a “Jamboard”, which is an interactive “smart” whiteboard. They have identified two distinct market segments for this product: Higher Ed (“H”), who can use this for teaching, and VC-Fueled Startups (“V”), who have more funding than they know what to do with. These segments have shown a lot of interest now that many workers are working remotely and many universities are doing distance learning. Fall demand for Jamboards for each subgroup is given by:


	Google estimates it has a constant marginal cost of $500 per Jamboard. 


	•	(4 points) What is the profit-maximizing price for a Jamboard to each group? 

Price to Higher Ed:

Price to VC-Fueled Startups:


Answer: $1250 to H, $4250 to V. Two points for each, one point if set up correctly but a math error. This comes from setting MR=MC for each of these (invert to get P= demand, multiply by Q to get revenue, take the derivative to get MR, set equal to 500). 


	•	(6 points) Google would be able to price discriminate in this way if it can verify who is buying a Jamboard and ask Higher Ed customers to prove their status. However, some clever startups may claim to be a Higher Ed institution to get a better price. What price would Google charge for a Jamboard if they could not verify a buyer’s type, e.g. if any startup could claim to be an institution of Higher Ed? 

Price for Jamboard: 

Answer: If you look at the combined demand curve, the answer looks like $2,250, HOWEVER, at that price, no H are buying. SO, the answer is 4250 (the optimal price for V). This is a bit tricky, since you need to check which part of the demand curve you are on after solving for the price of $2,250. You can verify by comparing charging a price of $2250 to only the V segment; that is clearly lower than charging $4250. (2 points for adding demand curves properly, 2 more points for getting to $2,250, 2 points for recognizing that $4,250 is actually the right price). 


	•	(5 points) An accounting firm has approached Google and offered to audit buyers who claim to be Higher Ed to confirm their true status, but they have asked for $1M for their services for the fall. Would Google be willing to pay this, and why? 

Answer: No, because profits don’t increase enough from the price discrimination.
problem_text:
Question 1: Price competition and capacity [30 points]

A-driving school and H-driving school are two competing driving schools in a town. Each can serve at most 80 students every week (and suppose each student takes a 1-hour training every week). The (weekly) cost of serving each student is $10.
The two driving schools compete on price. Each can choose an hourly rate: $50 or $40. Suppose each driving school aims to maximize its OWN weekly profit, and the schools set their prices simultaneously.
The following is the demand matrix which shows how many students want to choose each driving school at each possible price combination (before taking into account the capacity constraint). For example, if A charges $50 and H charges $40, then 40 students want to choose A and 110 students want to choose H.

                          H


A

$50
$40

$50
60, 75
40, 110

$40
75, 65
70, 90










Note that, due to the capacity constraint, H cannot always serve its entire demand. For example, when it has demand 110, it cannot serve 30 students. Half of those students who choose H but cannot be served will go to A-driving school and pay A’s price if A still has open seats. The other half will leave the market (for instance, because they do not like A-driving school and would rather to go to a driving school in the neighboring town). 

Given the current capacities, draw the (weekly) profit matrix of the price competition. Don’t forget the cost while computing profits! (You DON’T need to upload the matrix.) Then answer the following questions:

The effective DEMAND matrix, given H’s current capacity constraint is:
                       

                        H


A

$50
$40

$50
60, 75
55, 80

$40
75, 65
75, 80












Then the profit matrix is as follows:
                          H


A

$50
$40

$50
2400, 3000
2200, 2400

$40
2250, 2600
2250, 2400











(a): Does any driving school have a dominant strategy? [5 points]

H has a dominant strategy: $50.

(b) What prices will the two schools charge in the Nash equilibrium? [5 points]

Each driving school will charge $50. 

Suppose H-driving school now expands its capacity to 110. Suppose doing so involves a (weekly) fixed cost $200, but it does not affect the marginal cost. Draw a new profit matrix by yourself. (Again you DON’T need to upload the graph.) Then answer the following questions:

If H-driving schools expands its capacity to 110, the effective demand matrix will be the one provided initially, and the new payoff matrix will therefore be as follows (after taking into account the fixed cost $200:

                          H


A

$50
$40

$50
2400, 3000-200=2800
1600, 3300-200=3100

$40
2250, 2600-200=2400
2100, 2700-200=2500












(c) What prices will the two schools charge now? [5 points]

Now each school will charge $40. 

(d) How much (weekly) profit will H-driving school make? [5 points]

2500

The expansion will intensify price competition and drop its profit from 3000 to 2500. (Even without the expansion cost, its profit is only 2700, lower than before.)

(e) Suppose now that H-driving school also becomes a price leader after the capacity expansion, so that it sets its price first before A-driving school moves. In this case, how much (weekly) profit will H-driving school make? [5 points]

2800
In the sequential pricing game, backward induction predicts that H will charge $50, and A will follow and charge the same price. So H-driving school will make a profit 3000, the same as before expansion.

(f) Instead of expanding its capacity, H-driving school can alternatively sign a contract with A-driving school to buy its unused capacity.  By paying $15 for each student, H-driving school can send its unserved students to A-driving school (up to its capacity). For each of those students, A-driving school will then bear the cost $10. (Suppose A-driving school is always willing to accept this contract, and they set prices simultaneously afterwards.) Facing the three options: retain the status quo, expand capacity, or buy A’s unused capacity, which option will H-driving school choose? [5 points]

Remain the status quo.


If H-driving school signs that contract with A-driving school, the new payoff matrix in the price competition game will be:
                          H


A

$50
$40

$50
2400, 3000
1750, 3150

$40
2250, 2600
2150, 2650










[The payoff numbers in the two cells on the left are the same as before because the capacity constraints do not bind. The payoff numbers in the two cells on the right can be explained as follows:
For example, consider the cell when A charges $50 and H charges $40. In that case H will send 30 students to A. So H’s profit is 80*30+30*25=3150, because it makes a profit $30 from each of the 80 students served by itself and a profit $25 from each of the 30 students sent to A. A’s profit is 40*40+30*5=1750, because it makes a profit $40 from each of its own students and a profit $5 from each of the students from H. Similarly, the other cell on the right can be explained.]

From the payoff matrix, we can see that H has a dominant strategy $40, and anticipating that A will then choose $40 as well. So H will end up making a profit $2,650, which is still lower than the initial profit 3000.

given_solution:
Problem 2 (15 points) 

The COVID situation had led to much uncertainty in the travel industry. You are thinking about booking a trip over spring break in 2021, and the vacation package you are considering is to a Caribbean island and costs $3,000. You have $20,000 in savings and your utility function for your wealth is  where  is measured in thousands, so that your utility of $20,000 is .

The vacation package is non-refundable. However, you can purchase travel insurance for $750 that will refund your purchase if the US issues a travel advisory to this particular island covering spring break. You are not willing to violate a travel advisory, so if you purchase the vacation package without buying insurance and an advisory is issued, you will lose your $3,000. 

You estimate that there is a 20% chance of the US issuing a travel advisory that would prevent you from traveling over spring break. To be clear, you value the vacation at $3,000, so if you get to take the vacation, it is as if your wealth is $20,000, but if you pay for it and don’t get to take it, you lose the $3,000. 


	•	(4 points) Will you choose to buy this insurance? 

Answer: EU of taking risk: 

No, you will not buy this insurance. 

	•	(6 points) Is this insurance priced in an actuarially fair way (assuming the insurance company agrees with your 20% probability)? Would a person ever purchase insurance if it is not priced in an actuarially fair way? 

Answer: No. Expected cost is 0.2*3000=$600, but price is $750
BUT, people would buy if they are sufficiently risk averse. Full credit if they mention gains from trade between risk averse individuals and risk neutral firm; or, if they simply say that someone who is sufficiently risk averse would buy it. 

	•	(5 points) What is the most you would be willing to pay for this insurance policy?

Answer: There are a few ways to determine this. I will compute the certainty equivalent.

Therefore, the most I would pay is $619 (+/- 10 for rounding).
problem_text:
Question 2: A game of switching envelopes [15 points]
You and your friend are playing a game of switching envelopes. There are two envelopes, each containing an amount of money; the amount of money is either $2, $4, $8, $16, $32, or $64, and both of you know this. Furthermore, both of you are told that one envelope contains exactly twice as much money as the other. The two envelopes are shuffled, and one is given to you and the other is given to your friend. Both of you open your envelope secretly (so that the amounts inside are kept private), and then are given an opportunity to switch. If both of you agree to switch, you switch your envelopes; if at least one of you doesn’t want to switch, you keep your envelopes.
(a) If you see $16 in your envelope, should you offer to switch? [5 points]
No, you shouldn’t offer to switch.
This can be seen by a reasoning similar to backward induction. If a player receives an envelope with $64, she will never want to switch. Knowing that, a player who receives $32 should not offer to switch for the only time the other player might trade with her occurs when the other player has $16. Knowing that, a player who receives $16 shouldn’t offer to switch either.

(b) If you see $4 in your envelope, should you offer to switch? [5 points]
No. The same reasoning.

(c) Suppose the above underlined sentence is replaced by “both of you are told that the two envelopes contain different amounts of money”. If you see $8 in your envelope, should you offer to switch? [5 points]
No. That sentence doesn’t affect the reasoning qualitatively. A similar argument as before applies.









Section II: Short Questions [60 points]

Please be concise. None of the answers requires more than a few sentences.

	•	[10 points] The Indian government is contemplating the imposition of a price ceiling on an existing anti-viral drug that is proving to be effective in treating some of the symptoms of COVID-19. The patent for this drug has expired and the market for this drug is perfectly competitive, but the market is not at its long-run equilibrium since the COVID-19 situation is still evolving. Patient groups that typically advocate in favor of patient rights are vehemently opposed to this price ceiling. What is an economic rationale for their argument? 

5 points for noting that with the price ceiling, supply will fall and some patients will not be able to buy the drug who had been able to buy it before. 
5 points for noting that on the whole, patients will lose if supply is pretty elastic and providing a 

	•	[10 point] You are manufacturing cloth facemasks for your cohort at SOM. There are two designs: One is a fancy 3 ply mask with adjustable ear loops with a visual of the bulldog (the B-mask) and the other is a single-ply simple design in blue fabric with the Yale University Y (the Y-mask). You make these masks on a state-of-the-art sewing machine that you can use for free at the Yale Fabric Arts Studio.  In one hour on the machine, you can produce one fancy B-masks using a single worker who earns a wage of $15 per hour.  To make a Y-mask you require only thirty minutes on the machine, but for those thirty minutes you need two workers (at the same rate of $15 per hour) are required. For either type, a mask uses $5 worth of material. Since manufacturing Y or B masks takes $15 worth of labor, uses $5 worth of material and is done on a machine you can use for free, you are thinking of pricing them the same. Assuming you simply want to maximize profit, is this a good decision? Explain why, or why not. Provide some economic intuition, using numbers.

You cannot forget the opportunity cost of the machine.  For every hour of machine time spent on making Y-masks, you earn 2(py-$20).  For every hour spent on B-masks you make (pb-20).  Simplifying , if 2py-pb>20  you should make only Y-masks; if the  inequality goes the other way make B-masks and with the equality holding, the firm will be indifferent on product mix. 
Grading:  10 points . If someone knows there is an opportunity cost of the machine but has no idea how to get it give them 5 points. 
	•	[10 points] Oregon is considering imposing a high specific excise tax on manufacturers of cigarettes, cigars and vaping products. Voters will weigh in on this measure in November. Opponents of the tax make two arguments: First they claim that the proposed tax of $3.33 per package is too high and will drive companies out of business as they cannot afford to pay $3.33 out of the profit for every pack sold. Second, they argue that taxes are distortionary, and in this tax will create a lot of deadweight loss. What economic reasoning can you offer to counter the above two specific arguments?

5 points for saying incidence of tax shared between companies and consumers, and since cigarette demand is inelastic, burden of tax will be on consumers.
5 points for noting that taxes may be distortionary but since demand is inelastic, the extent of distortion is small. Or offering the argument that there are negative externalities – and so measuring deadweight loss just in the cigarette market is not a reasonable way to measure the impact of this tax.


	•	[10 points] You are in the market to buy a used car. Some sellers offer warranties and others don’t. You choose to go with a seller who offers a service warranty for two years, but with a $500 deductible. 
	•	Does offering a warranty address the problem of adverse selection, moral hazard, neither, or both?  
	•	Similarly, does the clause about the $500 deductible address the problem of adverse selection, moral hazard, neither, or both?  Explain your reasoning clearly
	•	Your classmate from SOM tells you about Carfax which is a website that provides information about whether a used car is low-quality or high-quality and what it is worth. Do you think such a service improves how the market functions? Under what conditions can such a service enable the used car market achieve the first-best outcome?

4 points for warranty: Addresses the adverse selection problem. Costly signal 
3 points for deductible: Addresses moral hazard. Helps prevent risk taking behavior by driver
3 points for saying yes, can improve. But first best only if services are free.

	•	[10 points] The market for Hawaiian Kona coffee is perfectly competitive. Your analysis of the financial statements of a large coffee producer in the region reveals that the firm has been making millions of dollars of profits consistently for the last ten years. Is this consistent with your understanding that long-run economic profits of a firm in a perfectly competitive market should be zero? Provide a precise explanation for your answer.

One of the following explanations
(1) Economic profits can be zero while accounting profits can still be positive. Financial statements show accounting profits – so ignores opportunity costs.
(2) When firms have economies of scale (e.g., when the ATC is U shaped), in the short run firms can earn a positive profit.
(3) Alternatively, marginal firms in a competitive market makes zero economic profit, but this firm is more efficient than the marginal ones.
Partial credit (5 points) for anything coherent about perfect competition. 

	•	[10 points] The Government of Japan imposes a very high import duty on copper products from Chile, Peru and Australia. Who do you think would be the gainers and losers in Japan from such legislation?  

(3 points) Producers of copper products in Japan would gain
(4 points) Consumers would pay higher prices and so would lose
(3 points) Government would get tax revenue
10 points for saying all three of the above points. OK if they add other things like employment in copper industry etc. But the above three points must be made.

Is this consistent with your understanding that long-run economic profits of a firm in a perfectly competitive market should be zero? Provide a precise explanation for your answer.

One of the following explanations
(1) Economic profits can be zero while accounting profits can still be positive. Financial statements show accounting profits – so ignores opportunity costs.
(2) When firms have economies of scale (e.g., when the ATC is U shaped), in the short run firms can earn a positive profit.
(3) Alternatively, marginal firms in a competitive market makes zero economic profit, but this firm is more efficient than the marginal ones.
Partial credit (5 points) for anything coherent about perfect competition. 

	•	[10 points] The Government of Japan imposes a very high import duty on copper products from Chile, Peru and Australia. Who do you think would be the gainers and losers in Japan from such legislation?  

(3 points) Producers of copper products in Japan would gain
(4 points) Consumers would pay higher prices and so would lose
(3 points) Government would get tax revenue
10 points for saying all three of the above points. OK if they add other things like employment in copper industry etc. But the above three points must be made.


Section III: Long Questions [75 points]

problem_text:
Problem 2 (15 points) 

The COVID situation had led to much uncertainty in the travel industry. You are thinking about booking a trip over spring break in 2021, and the vacation package you are considering is to a Caribbean island and costs $3,000. You have $20,000 in savings and your utility function for your wealth is  where  is measured in thousands, so that your utility of $20,000 is .

The vacation package is non-refundable. However, you can purchase travel insurance for $750 that will refund your purchase if the US issues a travel advisory to this particular island covering spring break. You are not willing to violate a travel advisory, so if you purchase the vacation package without buying insurance and an advisory is issued, you will lose your $3,000. 

You estimate that there is a 20% chance of the US issuing a travel advisory that would prevent you from traveling over spring break. To be clear, you value the vacation at $3,000, so if you get to take the vacation, it is as if your wealth is $20,000, but if you pay for it and don’t get to take it, you lose the $3,000. 


	•	(4 points) Will you choose to buy this insurance?
